--      The plane touched down with a little bump and taxied toward
--the terminal. Rose rummaged under the seat for our things. "You
--ready, Demos?" she said. "I'm ready, Rose."
--
--      We stepped into the aisle and set out together toward the next
--adventure.
--

--                Risk Analysis Checklist for Theraptly Platform
--            Prepared for internal platform assessment | 2026-01-27
--Background
--Theraptly is an AI-powered compliance platform designed to help behavioral-health facilities meet CARF and HIPAA standards. The platform supports uploading policy documents (PDF, DOC, DOCX), organizing them into compliance categories, and generating AI-driven reports that highlight gaps and recommendations ahead of audits. Because the platform may process protected health information (PHI) and other sensitive operational data, the risk analysis must cover regulatory compliance, data protection, AI-related risks, third-party dependencies, and operational controls.Key Risk Areas and Checklist
--Use this table to run a structured risk analysis of the Theraptly platform. Capture evidence for each check (screenshots, configurations, policy excerpts, logs) and record outcomes in a risk register.#Risk areaChecks and action itemsAssigned to1Data inventory and PHI mappingIdentify data collected: Document all information Theraptly collects (user account details, uploaded policies, metadata). Determine whether uploaded documents contain PHI or personally identifiable information.
--Map data flows: Outline how documents and metadata move through the platform (upload -> AI analysis -> storage -> download). Verify that only necessary data is processed and that PHI is minimized.
--Mr. Seyi (lead); You provide sample policies for testing2Access control and authenticationRole-based access control (RBAC): Confirm that Theraptly supports RBAC so only authorized users can view or edit certain documents. Evaluate permission granularity (admin vs staff).
--Password and MFA: Test password complexity requirements and check whether multi-factor authentication (MFA) is available and enforceable.
--Session management: Check for automatic session timeouts, secure cookies, and proper logout behavior.
--You test platform behavior; Mr. Seyi reviews documentation3Single sign-on (SSO) integrationVerify SSO integration methods (SAML, OAuth/OIDC) and confirm secure configuration (strong authentication, MFA enforcement, correct token/session handling, and least-privilege scopes).
--Mr. Seyi4Encryption and secure storageVerify encryption in transit and at rest (TLS everywhere; encryption of stored files and databases). Confirm key management approach (rotation, access restrictions, separation of duties).
--Confirm data residency and hosting controls (where data is stored, availability targets). Obtain evidence of relevant security attestations (e.g., SOC 2, ISO 27001) if claimed.
--Mr. Seyi5HIPAA and CARF complianceBusiness Associate Agreement (BAA): Ensure a BAA is signed. Review for permitted uses/disclosures, required safeguards, breach notification duties, subcontractor flow-down clauses, and data return/destruction terms.
--Minimum necessary access: Confirm the platform limits PHI access and only processes what is required for analysis.
--CARF coverage: Map Theraptly's categories (Policies and Procedures, Personnel and Workforce Management, Quality Management and Performance Improvement, Facility Health and Safety, Financial and Administrative, Program-Specific Compliance) to CARF standards to ensure coverage.
--Mr. Seyi6AI model transparency and accuracyAssess AI output quality: Upload sample policies and evaluate whether the AI report correctly identifies compliance gaps. Record false positives/negatives and potential bias patterns.
--Explainability and data use: Identify models used, what data is sent to the model provider, and whether any data is used for training (should be opt-in, contractually controlled, and disclosed).
--Human oversight: Confirm the platform supports human review, edits, and overrides of AI recommendations.
--You (testing) and Mr. Seyi (model and vendor review)7Document management and lifecycleFile scanning: Verify uploaded documents are scanned for malware before processing and storage.
--Version control: Check whether Theraptly maintains version history of policies and supports rollback.
--Retention and deletion: Determine retention periods, deletion functionality, and secure disposal approach.
--You test functions; Mr. Seyi reviews policies8Audit logging and monitoringConfirm audit logs exist for uploads, downloads, user access/actions, and AI-generated outputs. Logs should include who, what, when, and where (e.g., IP/device) as appropriate.
--Confirm log integrity protections (access controls, tamper resistance) and that regular reviews are performed with follow-up workflows.
--Mr. Seyi9Incident response and breach notificationReview incident response plan and confirm it includes detection, containment, investigation, evidence preservation, and notification steps relevant to HIPAA.
--Confirm mechanisms for users to report incidents and for internal escalation.
--Verify notification timelines and responsibilities (including subcontractor notification requirements).
--Mr. Seyi10Third-party and vendor managementIdentify all subprocessors (cloud hosting, AI services, email/SMS, analytics). Verify security posture and contracts (including BAA flow-down where PHI is involved).
--Collect vendor assurance evidence (SOC reports, penetration tests, security whitepapers) and track renewal dates.
--Establish continuous monitoring triggers (material changes, incidents, new vendors).
--Mr. Seyi11Governance, policies, and proceduresConfirm policies are documented, accessible, and version-controlled (security, privacy, access control, change management, incident response, retention).
--Verify operational procedures for onboarding, user provisioning, document upload/review, and approvals.
--Confirm there is an owner for each policy and a regular review cadence.
--Mr. Seyi12Training and awarenessEnsure role-based training exists for staff who use Theraptly (HIPAA basics, platform handling of sensitive data, secure practices).
--If using a training module inside Theraptly, confirm it can track completion, attestations, and evidence exports.
--You coordinate training; Mr. Seyi validates coverage13Usability and user experienceAssess whether the interface reduces cognitive load for staff and makes compliance tasks straightforward (upload, review, export).
--Collect feedback and document friction points.
--Check accessibility basics (keyboard navigation, screen reader considerations) and responsiveness.
--You conduct user testing; Mr. Seyi summarizes findings14Data subject rights and privacyVerify data export and deletion capabilities for organizations.
--Confirm processes to honor privacy requests where applicable (access, correction, deletion) and to support regulator inquiries.
--Mr. Seyi15Continuous improvement and reportingCompile a risk register from findings (risk statement, likelihood, impact, owner, remediation, due date, evidence).
--Set a review cadence (e.g., quarterly) and run re-assessments whenever major features or vendors change.
--Use platform analytics to monitor compliance trends and prioritize remediation.
--Mr. Seyi (lead); You supportSummary of Responsibilities
--You (platform walkthrough and validation)Create test accounts and walk through core flows (signup/login, organization setup, document upload, AI report view, export/download).
--Validate role-based access behavior and session handling (logout, timeout).
--Test AI output quality with sample policies and log errors, inaccuracies, or confusing recommendations.
--Run usability checks with 1-2 internal users and collect feedback on friction points.
--Coordinate training needs for internal staff using the platform.
--Mr. Seyi (compliance, security controls, and evidence pack)Review legal and compliance artifacts (BAA, privacy policy, terms, subprocessor list) and verify contractual HIPAA requirements.
--Verify technical controls: encryption, SSO configuration, audit logging, monitoring, and access management.
--Perform vendor/subprocessor due diligence and ensure required agreements are in place.
--Define incident response and breach notification expectations and validate Theraptly's processes align.
--Compile the risk register and remediation plan, with owners, due dates, and evidence.
--Reference Frameworks (for mapping and evidence)
--NIST SP 800-66r2: Implementing the HIPAA Security Rule (February 2024).
--AICPA Trust Services Criteria (2017, includes March 2020 updates) - useful for SOC 2-style control mapping.

--PRD
--Clinical Note Taker (PRD)     Detail
--                              Theraptly
-- Attribute                    Clinical Note Taker and Documentation
-- Product                      Automation
-- Feature Name                 1.0 (Initial Release)
--                              December 11, 2025
-- Version                      Licensed Clinical Therapists and Counselors
-- Date                         Draft
-- Target User
-- Status
--
--1. Goal and Success Metrics
--1.1 Goal
--
--To significantly reduce administrative burden for clinicians by automating the session
--documentation workflow, from secure recording and transcription to AI-driven SOAP/DAP note
--generation and seamless, compliant EHR integration.
--
--1.2 Success Metrics (KPIs)
--
--Metric                        Target
--Adoption Rate
--                              75% of active users utilize the note-taker for
--Time Saved                    at least one session per week within 3
--                              months of launch.
--EHR Transfer Success Rate
--Billing Code Acceptance Rate  15 minutes reduction in average
--Compliance                    documentation time per session, as
--                              measured by user feedback surveys.
--
--                              99.9% successful transfer rate of approved
--                              notes to integrated EHR systems.
--
--                              >95% acceptance rate of suggested
--                              CPT/ICD-10 codes by billing teams/systems.
--
--                              Zero PII/PHI exposure incidents; successful
--                              completion of SOC 2 Type 2 audit.
--2. Detailed Requirements
--2.1 Functional Requirements
--
--ID   Requirement              Description
--
--F-1  Secure Audio Recording The system must allow for secure, high-quality
--
--                              audio recording via both the Mobile App and a
--
--                              Telehealth Integration component.
--
--F-2  Consent Capture          A mandatory, auditable digital consent
--                              sign-off/verbal capture process must precede the
--                              recording start. The consent record must be
--                              securely time-stamped and linked to the session
--                              file.
--
--F-3  Transcription            Utilizes a Specialized ASR Model trained on
--                              medical terminology to generate a searchable
--                              transcript.
--
--F-4  Clinical Analysis        The system must use LLMs fine-tuned for clinical
--                              context to analyze the transcript, mapping dialogue
--                              to the structure of a clinical note (S, O, A, P).
--
--F-5  Note Generation          Automatically draft the final SOAP or DAP note
--                              structure based on the transcript analysis.
--
--F-6  Clinician Review Interface Provide a user-friendly web interface allowing the
--
--                              clinician to review, edit, and approve the draft note
--
--                              alongside the original transcript.
--
--F-7  Billing Code Suggestion  Generates a list of suggested CPT/ICD-10 codes
--
--                              based on the service type and documented
--
--                              assessment/plan.
--
--F-8  EHR Integration          The approved final note must be seamlessly and
--                              securely pushed to the clinician's designated EHR
--                              system. (See 2.2 Technical Requirements for
--                              integration method).
--2.2 Technical Requirements
--
--ID    Requirement          Description
--
--T-1   Data Encryption      All PHI/PII (audio, transcript, notes) must be secured with
--
--                           End-to-End Encryption (E2EE) in transit and at rest.
--
--T-2   Compliance           System architecture must be designed to meet all
--                           necessary requirements for HIPAA and SOC 2 Type 2
--      Foundation           compliance.
--
--T-3   EHR API Strategy     Initial integration will prioritize SMART on FHIR standards
--
--                           (where possible) for modern, secure data exchange with
--
--                           target EHRs (e.g., Epic, Cerner).
--
--T-4   Billing Validation   The billing code suggestion feature must incorporate a
--
--                           Rules Engine Layer to cross-validate suggestions against
--
--                           official coding guidelines before presentation.
--
--T-5   Audit Trail          Implement a comprehensive, immutable audit log that
--                           tracks every action on the data: recording start/stop,
--                           transcription, note edits, code review, and EHR transfer.
--
--T-6   AI Training Policy   Explicitly enforce a Zero-Data-Training Policy for client
--
--                           PHI. All model improvements must use synthetic or
--
--                           properly de-identified data.
--
--2.3 User Experience (UX) Requirements
--
--ID    Requirement           Description
--UX-1  Recording Indicator
--                            A clear, always-visible recording indicator in both
--                            the mobile app and telehealth integration to assure
--                            the user the session is being captured.
--UX-2  Review Flow         The note review interface (F-6) must allow easy
--                          side-by-side comparison of the original transcript
--UX-3  One-Click Approval  and the draft note for quick verification.
--
--                          The final step must be a clear, unambiguous
--                          "Approve and Submit to EHR" button, making the
--                          clinician responsible for the final output.
--
--3. Scope (Out of Scope for V1)
--
--    ●​ Real-time Live Correction/Coaching: The V1 focus is on post-session note generation,
--         not real-time assistance.
--
--    ●​ Billing Submission: We will suggest the codes and send the notes, but V1 will not handle
--         the actual submission or claims processing.
--
--    ●​ Integration with more than 3 target EHRs: Initial integration will be focused on the top 3
--         prioritized EHR systems.
--

--Question                          Dependency/Owner

--                                  only, or both initially?

--                                  What are the final legal sign-off requirements
--                                  for the BAA, consent workflow, and data
--                                  retention policies?

--Next Step Suggestion
--The next step in refining this PRD is to determine the target EHRs, as this defines the
--complexity and necessary detail for Epic 4: EHR Integration.
--Since I already offered to research this, let's proceed to get the necessary factual information.
--What are the top 3 EHR systems used by independent therapists and counselors that Theraptly
--should target first for integration? I will search for this information now.
--This detailed structure is much better suited for a development roadmap. The next critical step is
--to specify the EHR Integration requirements, which directly impacts the engineering effort.
--The research indicates that the most popular and relevant EHR systems for independent mental
--health practices are:
--* SimplePractice
--* TherapyNotes
--* TheraNest (now Ensora)
--Let's use these three as the primary targets for Epic 4.
--Here is the new Epic 4 structured with User Stories and Acceptance Criteria based on the
--industry standard of using FHIR APIs where possible for secure data exchange.
--
--Epic 4: Secure EHR Integration (V1 Targets: SimplePractice, TherapyNotes, TheraNest)
--(Goal: Seamlessly and securely transfer finalized clinical notes to the clinician's chosen
--EHR.)

--Epic 5: Compliance, Security, and Audit

--(Goal: Achieve and maintain HIPAA and SOC 2 compliance across the platform to ensure

--I can prevent unauthorized access or              Layer Security) for secure transmission (e.g.,
--disclosure.                                       recording upload, EHR transfer).

--Summary of PRD Epics (V1)
--
--Epic    Focus Area                      Goal
--Epic 1
--Epic 2  Session Capture & Consent       Securely capture session
--Epic 3                                  audio and mandatory
--Epic 4                                  consent.
--
--Epic 5  Core AI Processing              Transcribe, analyze, and
--                                        automatically draft clinical
--                                        notes.
--
--        Review, Finalization & Billing  Enable clinician review,
--                                        editing, final approval, and
--                                        code suggestion.
--
--        EHR Integration                 Securely connect and
--                                        transfer approved notes to
--                                        target EHRs (SimplePractice,
--                                        TherapyNotes, TheraNest).
--
--        Compliance & Audit              Implement mandatory HIPAA
--                                        and SOC 2 security controls,
--                                        encryption, and audit logging.
--HIPAA Compliance
--First, a critical framing point
--
--HIPAA compliance is not about whether data is “stored securely” in general terms. It is about:
--    ●​ Role-based access​
--    ●​ Minimum necessary principle​
--    ●​ Auditability​
--    ●​ Consent and authorization​
--    ●​ Clear separation of PHI vs non-PHI​
--    ●​ Data lifecycle control​
--
--Your flow is directionally reasonable, but several steps as written are non-compliant or
--underspecified.
--
--1. Admin view review
--
--Step 1: “Admin logs on to admin portal”
--
--Compliant if and only if:
--    ●​ Strong authentication (password + MFA recommended)​
--    ●​ Role is explicitly defined as Covered Entity workforce or Business Associate delegate​
--    ●​ Session timeouts are enforced​
--
--Missing but required:
--    ●​ Admin role definition (owner, supervisor, compliance officer)​
--    ●​ Audit logging of admin access​
--
--HIPAA requires you to prove who accessed what and when.
--Step 2: “Admin navigates to notetaker section”
--
--This is fine conceptually, but PHI exposure risk begins here.
--Requirements:
--
--    ●​ Admin view must be configurable to limit PHI visibility​
--    ●​ Many admins should not see raw session content​
--    ●​ Default should be metadata-first, content-second​
--
--A common failure:
--
--     Admin dashboards that show full clinical notes by default
--
--That violates minimum necessary access.
--
--Step 3: “Admin creates/adds clinicians”
--
--This is compliant only if:
--    ●​ Clinicians are provisioned as workforce members​
--    ●​ Each clinician has a unique user ID​
--    ●​ No shared credentials​
--    ●​ Access is scoped to assigned patients only​
--
--Risk area:
--    ●​ Sending credentials by email​
--
--Emailing credentials is not acceptable unless:
--    ●​ It is a one-time activation link​
--    ●​ It expires​
--    ●​ It forces password creation on first login​
--
--Plain-text credentials over email are a red flag.
--
--Step 4: “Admin creates/adds patients”
--
--This is a major PHI boundary.
--Creating a patient record means:
--
--    ●​ You are storing identifiers​
--    ●​ You are now handling PHI, not just audio​
--
--Required clarifications:
--    ●​ What identifiers are stored at creation?​
--    ●​ Is this MRN, initials, alias, or full demographic data?​
--    ●​ Who is authorized to create patients?​
--
--Best practice:
--    ●​ Patient records should support pseudonymization​
--    ●​ Admins should not be required to enter full PHI to enable notetaking​
--
--Step 5: “Admin views session data”
--
--This is the highest-risk step.
--HIPAA principle violated by default:
--
--    ●​ Admin ≠ clinician​
--    ●​ Admin does not automatically have the right to read notes​
--
--You must implement:
--    ●​ Read-only vs full access separation​
--    ●​ Explicit access grants​
--    ●​ Purpose-based access (audit, supervision, billing)​
--
--Without this, enterprise buyers will reject you.
--
--2. Clinician view review
--
--Step 1: “Clinician downloads app”
--
--This is fine.
--But:
--
--    ●​ You must assume the device is personal​
--    ●​ You cannot assume device-level encryption is adequate​
--    ●​ You must design for device loss or compromise​
--
--This affects caching and offline behavior.
--
--Step 2: “Launches app”
--
--At this point:
--    ●​ No PHI should be accessible​
--    ●​ No residual data should be present​
--    ●​ No cached audio should load​
--This must be explicit in your threat model.
--
--Step 3: “Signs up with credentials sent to email”
--
--This is problematic as stated.
--Acceptable pattern:
--
--    ●​ Admin sends invitation email​
--    ●​ Clinician clicks secure link​
--    ●​ Clinician sets password​
--    ●​ MFA optional but strongly recommended​
--
--Unacceptable pattern:
--    ●​ Username + password sent via email​
--
--This will fail any serious HIPAA review.
--
--Step 4: “Sets up session”
--
--Critical questions:
--    ●​ How is the patient selected?​
--    ●​ Is patient identity visible or coded?​
--    ●​ Is consent handled?​
--
--HIPAA does not mandate written consent for treatment, but:
--    ●​ Recording audio raises state and country consent laws​
--    ●​ You need explicit product-level consent tracking​
--Step 5: “Records session”
--
--This is the single most sensitive action.
--Required safeguards:
--
--    ●​ Clear recording indicator​
--    ●​ Ability to pause or stop​
--    ●​ Explicit consent confirmation (even if one-time per patient)​
--    ●​ Encryption in transit and at rest​
--    ●​ No background recording​
--
--Also:
--    ●​ You must define whether raw audio is stored, for how long, and why​
--
--Step 6: “Session information (notes, patient) is recorded on admin
--dashboard”
--
--As written, this is non-compliant.
--Correct framing:
--
--    ●​ Session artifacts are stored in the system​
--    ●​ Visibility depends on role and permissions​
--    ●​ Admin dashboard should default to summaries and status, not content​
--
--You need a visibility matrix, not a flat dashboard.
--
--3. Missing but mandatory elements
--These are not optional.
--
--1. Audit logs
--
--    ●​ Every access to PHI​
--    ●​ Every export​
--    ●​ Every role change​
--
--2. Data retention controls
--
--    ●​ Audio retention duration​
--    ●​ Note retention duration​
--    ●​ Deletion policies​
--
--HIPAA does not mandate deletion timelines, but customers will.
--
--3. PHI boundary clarity
--
--You must define:
--    ●​ What is PHI​
--    ●​ When data becomes PHI​
--    ●​ When it stops being PHI​
--
--This affects analytics, AI training, and logging.
--
--4. Business Associate Agreements
--
--    ●​ You must execute BAAs with customers​
--    ●​ Your cloud vendors must sign BAAs with you​
--Without this, nothing else matters.
--
--4. Summary assessment
--
--What is solid
--
--    ●​ Clear role separation intent​
--    ●​ Centralized admin oversight​
--    ●​ Clinician-first workflow​
--    ●​ Reasonable high-level flow​
--
--What must be fixed
--
--    ●​ Credential delivery method​
--    ●​ Admin access to session content​
--    ●​ Patient identity handling​
--    ●​ Consent and recording controls​
--    ●​ Explicit auditability​
--
--Bottom line
--
--Your journey is directionally correct but not HIPAA-ready as written.
--With modest but precise changes:
--
--    ●​ Invitation-based access​
--    ●​ Role-scoped visibility​
--    ●​ Metadata-first dashboards​
--    ●​ Explicit consent handling​
--
--It can be made defensible.
--If you want, the next step should be one of:
--
--    ●​ A role-permission matrix​
--    ●​ A PHI data flow diagram​
--    ●​ A redlined “HIPAA-safe” version of this journey​
--    ●​ Or a checklist enterprise buyers will use to reject or approve this flow​
--
--Say which one you want to do next.
--

--2025
--Healthcare
--Compliance
--Outlook
--
--          Emerging Cybersecurity, Data
--          and Regulatory Risk in the Age of AI
--
--                                                                                                                                                                        1
--Executive
--Summary
--
--Compliance is a critical line of defense for healthcare organizations as they navigate
--complex regulations, mounting cyberattacks and rapid-fire technological change. Yet the
--majority of U.S. compliance professionals surveyed in Barnes & Thornburg’s inaugural
--Healthcare Compliance Outlook report are stretched thin and feeling less than confident
--as new risks – including those associated with the explosive growth of artificial intelligence
--(AI) – add to their workload. That concern may be warranted: our research identifies several
--missed opportunities that could deepen healthcare organizations’ exposure to risk.
--
--Barnes & Thornburg             Respondents included CEOs, chief compliance officers, chief
--surveyed 120 compliance,       risk officers and in-house counsel, all of whom either lead or
--risk and legal leaders across  support compliance and risk matters within their organizations.
--U.S.-based healthcare and      Organizations ranged in size from entities with less than $1
--life sciences organizations,   million in annual revenue to those generating $1 billion to more
--including hospital systems,    than $10 billion each year.
--physicians’ practices, and
--pharmaceutical, biotech        Our research looked at the hurdles facing healthcare
--and medical device             compliance professionals in the year ahead and examined
--manufacturers.                 how their organizations are addressing high-risk areas. We
--                               also explored how the industry is currently using AI for internal
--                               legal compliance functions and what steps organizations are
--                               taking to ensure proper governance and oversight.
--
--                                                                                                2
--                                                                     More than half of respondents report resource
--                                                                     constraints in compliance program areas like
--                                                                     budget, staffing and technology, all while costs
--                                                                     continue to grow as healthcare organizations
--                                                                     incorporate AI into their operations. These stresses
--                                                                     could potentially affect care delivery: only 42% are
--                                                                     very confident when it comes to maintaining high
--                                                                     quality of care in light of compliance and risk issues.
--                                                                     Significantly, in this constrained fiscal environment,
--                                                                     more than half (54%) of respondents say their
--                                                                     organizations have either accepted, are seeking
--                                                                     or are considering private equity (PE) backing.
--                                                                     And more than a quarter (27%) of those who aren’t
--                                                                     currently considering PE backing say they would
--                                                                     do so in the future, highlighting the increased
--                                                                     importance of private capital.
--
--Our AI findings were revealing, underscoring that health risk and compliance professionals – like
--those in other industries – are setting up AI-related policies in a relative vacuum as the use of tools like
--generative AI outpaces the development of regulatory frameworks. Beyond compliance reports, there
--is no consensus among organizations that have already implemented AI on other measures to guide its
--ethical use. Despite that, nearly three-quarters of respondents are either already leveraging AI in their
--internal legal compliance functions or are considering it, with organizations embracing both predictive
--and generative AI for data analysis, risk assessments, administrative tasks and other uses.
--
--With these and other compliance pressures looming, such as proliferating data privacy requirements,
--our overall findings suggest organizations may not be using all the tools at their disposal. Just 48% of
--respondents say their organizations currently audit high-risk areas and even fewer collaborate with
--external industry, legal or compliance partners or regulatory bodies as part of their compliance and risk-
--mitigation strategies. Considering all of this, it’s not surprising that less than one in three say they are
--“very prepared” to meet future compliance and risk challenges.
--
--Overall, we found that while healthcare risk professionals are aware of the hazards around AI integration,
--data privacy and other compliance hotspots, organizations could be doing more to proactively safeguard
--their businesses and support patient health. In what follows, we’ll explore those findings in greater
--detail and offer guidance to help healthcare risk, compliance and legal leaders adapt to this dynamic
--regulatory environment against a backdrop of mounting risk and limited resources.
--
--                                                                                                                                                                        3
--Key Findings
--
--Compliance
--
--53%                            More than half (53%) of healthcare compliance
--                               and risk leaders report resource constraints in
--                               program areas like budget, staffing and technology;
--                               56% predict forthcoming limitations
--
--31%                             42%
--
--Only 31% feel “very prepared”  Less than half (42%) are “very confident”
--to meet future compliance      about maintaining high quality of care in
--and risk challenges            light of compliance and risk issues
--
--54%                            More than half (54%) of organizations are
--                               already seeking PE backing or considering it
--
--48%                            Less than half (48%) audit high-risk areas, and
--                               even fewer currently collaborate with external
--                               industry, legal or compliance partners or
--                               proactively engage with regulators as part
--                               of their compliance and risk mitigation
--
--                                                                                                                                      4
--Key Findings
--
--AI
--
--58%           Nearly 3/4 of respondents are using or
--              considering using AI – both generative and
--              predictive – in their internal legal compliance
--              function; data analysis, risk assessments
--              and administrative tasks are the most
--              popular applications
--
--              >10%
--
--              Six in 10 of those respondents say that AI
--              integration and development will add more
--              than 10% to their budget in the coming year
--
--              58% of all respondents say developing a
--              governance structure for AI compliance is
--              difficult. Beyond compliance reports, there is
--              no consensus among organizations that have
--              already implemented AI on what measure to
--              guide its ethical use
--
--                                                               5
--Compliance and
--Risk Landscape
--
--From costly ransomware attacks to evolving guidance around cybersecurity under the Health Insurance
--Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA), the healthcare industry is confronting mounting risk and
--compliance responsibilities heading into 2025.
--
--Only 4% of respondents say the current compliance and risk issues facing their organizations do not
--concern them – and fewer than one in three say they are “very prepared” to meet future compliance
--and risk challenges. How those challenges evolve could depend, in part, on the 2024 presidential
--election, given the two candidates’ divergent views on regulation. Regardless of the outcome, effective
--compliance programs will continue to be critical for minimizing corporate risk.
--
--Sufficient defense against cyberattacks is casting a particularly long shadow over the industry amid
--fallout from recent incidents like the Change Healthcare data breach. Fifty-six percent of respondents
--cite external cybersecurity/data management/privacy risks as a concern for the coming year, with
--similarly high levels of concern surfacing around the threat of data ransom attacks (52%) and HIPAA/
--patient data breaches (49%). Internal cybersecurity/data management/data privacy events and regulatory
--changes also ranked as key concerns, cited by 48% and 47%, respectively.
--
--Some organizations may be less equipped than others to navigate the above challenges, whether that’s
--due to resource limitations, the size of their internal risk and compliance teams – half (50%) have five or
--fewer employees on such teams – or the degree to which they collaborate with external industry, legal
--or compliance partners or proactively engage with regulators.
--
--                  As it relates to cybersecurity, how would you rate your level
--               of concern regarding the following issues in the upcoming year?
--
--56%            52%
--

--                                                                                                                         6
--Compliance priorities and pressures
--
--Given these responses, it tracks that security and privacy top the list of priorities for healthcare
--organizations, selected by two-thirds (66%) of those citing concerns about risk and compliance issues.
--Auditing high-risk compliance areas (48%) is next, followed by staying up to date with emerging federal/
--state regulations (45%) amid a raft of privacy, quality and safety requirements that affect everything from
--provider referrals (e.g., the Stark Law and Anti-Kickback Statute) to telehealth and how organizations bill for
--and code medical procedures, conduct laboratory tests, and store and distribute controlled substances.
--
--Enhancing/updating compliance documents and frameworks is also a concern (39%). As for organizations
--that are conducting audits, they are most focused on data privacy and security (63%), with a much lower
--share doing them for areas such as fraud, waste and abuse prevention (26%) or patient privacy (22%).
--
--Which of the following issues represent current priorities for your organization?
--                                      (Select all that apply)
--
--Security and privacy                                                                       66%
--
--Auditing high-risk compliance areas                                                   48%
--
--Staying up to date with emerging federal and state regulations                   45%
--
--Enhancing and updating compliance documents and frameworks                          39%
--                                           Addressing resource limitations       32%
--
-- Addressing sta ng challenges                                                    32%
--Enhancing knowledge/expertise                                                    32%
--
--Limiting or preventing government investigations and fraud/abuse claims     29%
--
--Conducting enterprise-wide compliance reviews                               27%
--
--Budget/cost controls                                                        27%
--
--Aligning with other control functions                                       18%
--
--Voluntary self-disclosure of misconduct                                     18%
--
--   * Question was asked to those who indicated the current compliance
--and risk issues facing their organization are at least somewhat concerning
--
--Notably, less than half of respondents with compliance and risk concerns see such audits as a priority,
--which ties back to another theme in our research: the constraints facing healthcare risk and compliance
--programs. The majority (53%) of survey respondents say they are experiencing resource limitations in
--their compliance programs, and 56% expect to encounter them over the next year. Budgetary pressure
--is the leading limitation, with five in 10 of the above groups citing constraints on financial resources,
--followed by those relating to skilled talent and technological resources (44% and 38%, respectively).
--
--Organizations anticipate more trouble ahead on this front in the coming year, with staffing issues/talent
--shortage ranking as the top concern (50%), followed by financial constraints (46%). As a result, 32% of
--respondents say addressing these resource limitations and staffing challenges is a priority.
--
--                                                                                                           7

--                                                                                                      8
--Teaming up to mitigate risk
--
--One strategy that could help resource-strapped leaders offset these constraints is teaming up with
--internal or external groups beyond their own departments to buttress compliance efforts. However,
--less than half (44%) of respondents are currently doing so, and one-third have not considered such
--partnerships to date. While roughly one in four healthcare organizations we surveyed collaborates
--externally with industry associations (43%) or legal experts (40%), only 35% are hiring compliance
--consultants – and just 30% are proactively working with regulatory bodies.
--
--The industry could be missing out on a vital source of support by failing to embrace cross-functional
--teamwork and external collaborations. And the stakes are high. We asked respondents how confident
--their organizations are, in light of their compliance and risk priorities, in maintaining high quality of care –
--a key issue for the industry. Less than half (42%) said they are “very confident.”
--
--Which of the following actions have been implemented or considered
--    by your organization as compliance and risk mitigation tactics?
--
--Ongoing training and education                                    73%
--
--Liability and malpractice insurance                               72%
--
--Regular updates to policies, procedures and frameworks       67%
--
--Data security measures                                       65%
--
--Compliance audits and monitoring                             58%
--
--Voluntary self-disclosure of misconduct                      54%
--
--External collaboration with industry associations       43%
--
--External collaboration with legal experts               40%
--
--Hiring compliance consultants                           35%
--
--Collaboration with external stakeholders                31%
--
--Proactive collaboration with regulatory bodies          30%
--
--Partnering with private equity
--
--Amid these headwinds, it’s perhaps not surprising that the industry is looking to additional sources of
--capital for support. The healthcare sector is drawing interest from PE due to the aging population and
--increased rate of chronic diseases; as innovation in drugs and devices advances, so does the need for
--funding to improve efficiency and services. More than half of respondents (54%) say their organizations
--have either accepted PE backing (22%), are actively seeking it (14%), are in the process of negotiating
--to receive it (14%), or are considering it as an option (4%). More than a quarter (27%) of those who aren’t
--currently considering PE backing say they would do so in the future, highlighting the increased importance
--of private capital in the current healthcare landscape.
--
--                                                                                                             9
--To be sure, PE involvement in healthcare has drawn increased scrutiny in recent months as antitrust and
--other federal and state regulators examine how consolidation influences the quality of and access to care,
--patient health and medical workers. Healthcare PE deals must navigate a complex landscape of federal
--and state regulations beyond antitrust – including the Anti-Kickback Statute, self-referral prohibitions
--and the corporate practice of medicine doctrines – and ensuring compliance is critical to avoid legal,
--financial and reputational risks. However, such “buy-and-build” strategies can offer significant benefits for
--organizations, and a number of deals have moved forward despite challenges.
--
--Which of the following represents your organization's status
--when it comes to receiving backing from private equity (PE)

--
--                                                                                            10
--Compliance and risk guidance
--
--                     Cyberattacks
--
--                          Unfortunately, we expect that ransomware and phishing attacks will only increase
--                          in the coming year, so it’s critical to be prepared on all fronts. Companies should
--                          consider continued investment in effective threat-detection tools, including
--                          AI-powered ones. To comply with evolving federal and state requirements,
--                          organizations should employ strong incident response plans and conduct
--                          regular simulations and employee training.
--
--                     Compliance priorities
--
--                          Healthcare organizations should consider implementing continuous monitoring
--                          systems and controls to keep pace with rapidly changing regulations. It’s
--                          important to conduct proactive internal audits to identify risks and address
--                          potential compliance gaps.
--
--                     Private Equity
--
--                          As PE investment in healthcare accelerates, organizations considering such
--                          opportunities should anticipate increased scrutiny and oversight at the state
--                          and federal levels. Organizations considering a PE-backed deal should ensure
--                          they conduct thorough due diligence and comply with complex regulatory and
--                          enforcement requirements.
--
--                                                                                                                                                                       11
--AI Implementation
--and Ethical Use
--
--Like the rest of the business world, the healthcare industry is ablaze with AI fever; like businesses across
--the spectrum, the healthcare industry must weigh the potential advantages against concerns around
--accuracy, data privacy and more. Current conversations touch on everything from HIPAA and third-party
--vendor contracts to whether to build or buy AI-assisted technology – plus what proper oversight and
--governance look like as organizations proceed with AI investments.
--
--Our research shows that multiple C-suite roles may take the lead in such matters, with the CEO front and
--center (56%). Interestingly, while half of the organizations we surveyed report their compliance teams
--include a chief compliance officer, only one in three said the chief compliance officer takes a leading role
--in AI matters. This mixed picture may be due in part to the current dynamics around generative AI, where
--companies see the nascent technology as a business imperative but are also wary of the associated
--risks. CEOs may be leading on generative AI while compliance and risk-oriented C-suite roles oversee
--predictive AI, which is more mature.
--
--Generative and predictive AI in healthcare compliance
--
--Generative AI                                    Predictive AI
--
--Generates new content, such as images, text or   Utilizes machine learning and historical data
--music, based on patterns learned from existing   to forecast future outcomes or trends (e.g., risk
--data (e.g., training content/policy generation,  assessments, fraud and anomaly detection).
--scenario simulation, system testing).
--
--Nearly three-fourths of respondents say their organizations are either using or considering using AI – both
--generative and predictive – in their internal legal compliance functions. Generative AI has a slight edge,
--with 31% having already implemented it, compared to 28% for predictive AI, which is more specialized and
--less easily applied to basic tasks.
--
--Data analysis, risk assessments and administrative tasks are the top ways organizations are leveraging or
--considering leveraging both generative and predictive AI. The former appears to have deeper penetration
--across multiple tasks, potentially because of the relative ease of launching or customizing generative AI
--solutions – essentially, using ChatGPT for various functions – via application programming interfaces (APIs).
--
--                                                                                                    12
--  Ways organizations have leveraged or                                        Ways organizations have leveraged or
--are considering leveraging generative AI                                      are considering leveraging predictive AI
--
--57%                                                     Data analysis                                 50%
--        45%                                          Risk assessments                        37%
--         43%                                       Administrative tasks
--         43%                              Training, education and evaluation                   39%
--            39%                                     Medical diagnostics               28%
--                     27%                                                          22%
--                     25%                                    Sta ng             17%
--                     25%                        Research & development         18%
--                         20%                   Cost-e ectiveness analysis          24%
--                                                                              16%
--                                                        Telemedicine
--
--With that said, the jury is still out on how generative AI can be used for internal legal compliance functions.
--Predictive AI has the longer track record, including in the regulatory risk assessments that are central to
--many compliance teams’ work. Of respondents who are using it for that function, 40% say predictive AI is
--“effective” and 17% rate it “very effective.”
--
--As for where they’re sourcing these tools, 41% of respondents are commissioning a solution from external
--sources, with a surprisingly robust 36% choosing to develop them in-house. One-third are buying a
--preexisting AI solution, and 32% are adapting a proprietary version derived from publicly available
--technology – which means organizations need to rigorously evaluate the risks and terms attached to those
--tools, such as who owns the data entered into these systems and how these tools are trained.
--
--However, those paths diverge to some degree depending on the type of AI, with a slightly higher percentage
--of predictive AI implementers opting to outsource compared to generative AI (57% versus 52%); conversely,
--fewer predictive AI implementers are adapting publicly available technology (30% versus 36%).
--
--Governance and oversight
--
--With potential applications for AI multiplying even as regulatory frameworks are still being devised,
--compliance and risk leaders acknowledge the challenges ahead. Fifty-eight percent of respondents say
--that developing a governance structure for AI compliance in their organization is difficult at present, with
--52% anticipating some degree of difficulty over the next 12 months.
--
--Asked what measures are most important to maintain ethical standards around AI use, the majority
--of respondents (53%) said frequent risk assessments. These assessments far outpace other options,
--including ongoing monitoring and evaluation (37%) and internal training and education efforts (34%). Some
--organizations are also conducting legal and policy reviews for new AI regulations on a monthly basis.
--
--                                                                                                                        13
--Some healthcare organizations may not have much in the way of AI guardrails at all. This may be in part
--because there are few regulations yet that would push organizations to create them – another area
--where developments over the next year could depend to some degree on the 2024 election results. But
--while compliance efforts are often reactive, all risk, compliance, and legal professionals using AI in their
--organizations must be proactive. That means setting up policies, guardrails, and procedures and guidelines
--for use, despite the largely nascent state of AI-specific regulation.
--
--Our findings suggest that when it comes to AI, industry professionals are essentially operating in silos, with
--little visibility into how peers are approaching the technology. Outside of compliance reports – which 51% of
--respondents cite – there seems to be no consensus among organizations that have already implemented
--AI on other measures to guide its ethical use. Though many use risk assessments or internal audits (46%),
--data security and privacy measures (41%) and risk management guidelines (38%), the general lack of
--concrete policy is somewhat surprising.
--
--Perhaps this is due to the emergent stage of AI at many healthcare organizations, with some likely more in
--exploration mode as opposed to leveraging the technology in a meaningful way.
--
--   You indicated that your organization is currently leveraging AI in its functions. What compliance
--measures does your organization have in place to ensure the ethical use of AI? (Select all that apply)
--
--                                    Compliance report                                                  51%
--                                       Risk assessment                                          46%
--                                        Internal audit(s)                                       46%
--                                                                                        41%
--             Data security and privacy measures                                      38%
--                      Risk management guidelines                                 35%
--                                                                              32%
--           Internal training and education e orts                             32%
--Legal and policy review for new AI regulations                                32%
--                                                                              32%
--          External training and education e orts
--    Ongoing monitoring and evaluation e orts                             24%
--                                                                     22%
--                 Cybersecurity tabletop exercises                    22%
--                          Accountability frameworks                  22%
--                                                                     22%
--          Bolstering cybersecurity and IT e orts                  19%
--      Ethical oversight committee/review board                    19%
--                                                                  19%
--                                     Incident reporting           19%
--                 Incident management guidelines               16%
--        Bias or inaccuracy prevention measures             14%
--                                                           14%
--                    Vendor compliance guidelines
--                                      Data governance
--
--                      Informed consent for patients
--        Transparency around AI algorithm basis
--
--                 Alignment with clinical guidelines
--                           Stakeholder engagement
--
--* Question was asked to those who have already
-- implemented generative AI and/or predictive AI
--
--                                                                                                            14
--Top AI challenges and costs
--
--AI-related pressures on healthcare compliance extend beyond governance issues and the hunt for
--suitable applications. Respondents say the top challenges for AI implementation are data privacy issues
--(33%), compliance adherence (29%) and data breaches (26%) – consistent with the challenges for risk and
--compliance programs as a whole.
--
--AI could also have real implications for the bottom line. Of those organizations that are either implementing
--it or considering doing so, six in 10 expect their budgets for AI integration and development will increase by
--more than 10% in the next year – and one-fifth anticipate a more than 30% jump. That range could reflect
--the somewhat haphazard approach many companies are taking with AI; setting out AI-related line items
--may be less common than simply tallying up expenses that occur during real-time experimentation and
--trying to account for them after the fact.
--
--Regardless, those anticipated costs come at a pivotal time for healthcare risk and compliance leaders who
--are being asked to do more with less, even as their remit broadens. Given those constraints, it’s critical
--that professionals use all the tools at their disposal – including using cross-functional teams and proactive
--collaboration with outside resources – to keep pace.
--
--What measures do you believe are most important to maintain
--     ethical standards around AI use? (Select all that apply)
--
--                      Regular risk assessments                                                 53%
--Ongoing monitoring and evaluation e orts
--                                                                                          37%
--      Internal training and education e orts                                           34%
--                         Ongoing internal audits                                  30%
--                                                                                29%
--    Ongoing training and education e orts                                       29%
--                   Periodic compliance reports                             25%
--                                                                        22%
--         Data security and privacy measures
--     External training and education e orts                        15%
--                                                                 14%
--                 Risk management guidelines                    13%
--            Cybersecurity tabletop exercises                   13%
--                                                               13%
--                                Incident reporting          11%
--                                 Data governance           10%
--     Bolstering cybersecurity and IT e orts                10%
--                 Informed consent for patients           8%
--            Alignment with clinical guidelines           8%
--                     Accountability frameworks         7%
-- Ethical oversight committee/review board              7%
--   Bias or inaccuracy prevention measures             6%
--                      Stakeholder engagement        4%
--         Transparency around AI algorithms
--               Vendor compliance guidelines
--            Incident management guidelines
--
--                                                                                                         15
--AI guidance
--
--                     Plan before you act
--
--                          Organizations need to have governance policies in place before they launch AI
--                          tools, paying particular attention to data privacy and security safeguards as AI
--                          technologies penetrate deeper into risk assessment and compliance monitoring.
--                          Healthcare companies must review AI tools before they experiment with them to
--                          ensure they understand the rules for use and any limitations that such platforms
--                          may place on outputs, including whether commercial use to develop new
--                          products and services is permitted.
--
--                     Governance
--
--                          As AI regulations continue to take shape across jurisdictions, organizations should
--                          implement robust frameworks that align with emerging regulatory standards, such
--                          as the EU AI Act, and with established AI ethical principles. Healthcare companies
--                          should also consider participation in industry initiatives to share best practices.
--
--                     Challenges and costs
--
--                          The rapid pace of AI-related change means that legal and regulatory regimes will
--                          often be a step behind as new tools and capabilities proliferate. Organizations
--                          must prepare for increased compliance costs tied to emerging regulatory
--                          requirements like third-party audits, with the expectation that additional expenses
--                          will arise as the technology advances.
--
--                                                                                                                                                                       16
--Methodology
--and Demographics
--
--Barnes & Thornburg surveyed 120 compliance
--and risk leaders with the help of Dynata, a third-
--party B2B panel provider. The online survey was
--conducted in May 2024.
--The respondents included CEOs, chief compliance
--officers, chief risk officers and in-house counsel,
--all of whom played a leading or supporting role
--in their organizations’ risk and compliance matters.
--Organizational types included hospital systems,
--physicians’ practices, and pharmaceutical, biotech
--and medical device manufacturers, all based in
--the U.S.
--Organizational size and gross revenues also
--varied, with nearly half of the respondents
--reporting annual revenue above $10 million,
--and 10% above $1 billion.
--Responses were anonymous and data was
--analyzed in the aggregate. Due to rounding
--and questions to which more than one response
--was allowed, data may not add up to 100%.
--
--                                                                                                                                                                       17

--and Demographics

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
--2025 Retrospective
--Product Lead  --  2025 Retrospective 
--1) Overall Goal
--1.1 What was Product's primary goal for 2025?
--Stated intent (what we thought we were doing):
--Ship an MVP Policy Writer within 2 - 4 weeks of design kickoff that clients could use to upload policies and receive a gap analysis.
--What this actually was: A speed-based delivery plan, not a product outcome.
--
--1.2 Was that goal explicit and measurable?
--Explicit? Partially.
--The goal was explicit. But several factors including development cadence/commitment, migration, security, scope creep, poor product definition, discovering requirements late, HIPAA/SOC, working on MHRS service separately led to increased delivery time.
--Measurable? Weakly measurable.
--  oo   The only implied metric was "did we ship something quickly?"
--  oo   There was no definition of:
--     oo      what "online" meant
--     oo      what accuracy meant
--     oo      what "usable" meant
--     oo      what counted as success with real clients
--There was no single binary success condition.
--
--1.3 Did we achieve it?
--Status: Not at all.
--Evidence:
--  oo   Policy writer:
--     oo      ❌ Not in production
--     oo      ❌ Not tested end-to-end
--     oo      ❌ Not used by real clients
--     oo      ❌ No automated gap analysis that can be trusted
--  oo   Customers:
--     oo      ❌ No paying facilities using the policy writer
--     oo      ❌ No completed delivery cycle
--  oo   Compliance:
--     oo      ❌ Hipaa not understood early
--     oo      ❌ Risk analysis not started (infra instability)
--
--1.4 Why or why not? (Specific, not circumstantial)
--Decision failures
--  oo   We optimized for speed of shipping instead of truth (working ai), defensibility, and delivery
--  oo   We did not understand early that:
--     oo      compliance would gate architecture
--     oo      accuracy would gate sales
--  oo   We allowed "Mvp" to mean "anything that runs"
--Scope failures
--  oo   Massive scope creep without a kill rule:
--     oo      editor
--     oo      separate gap analysis
--     oo      Carf plans
--  oo   Multiple features were built before one was fully usable
--Execution failures
--  oo   No locked definition of done
--  oo   No binary milestones
--  oo   No fallback delivery lane
--  oo   No delivery ownership model
--Compliance discovery
--  oo   Hipaa + sud Part 2 implications were discovered late
--  oo   This forced re-architecture and invalidated early assumptions
--Dependency failures
--  oo   Multiple infra moves (Contabo --> Aws --> Gcp)
--  oo   No stable environment long enough to harden the product
--  oo   Slow engineering velocity relative to scope
--
--2) Process Review
--2.1 What are the current core processes in Product?
--Actual processes (not idealized):
--  oo   Ceo conducts research and customer conversations
--  oo   Ceo synthesizes insights mentally
--  oo   Ceo is final decision-maker on:
--     oo      what to build
--     oo      when to build
--     oo      whether something ships
--  oo   Product documentation:
--     oo      a few videos
--     oo      a few documents
--     oo      heavy reliance on memory
--  oo   Build starts quickly with unclear finish criteria
--  oo   Scope expands reactively as new ideas emerge
--
--2.2 Which processes consistently worked?
--  oo   Ceo-led customer conversations
--     oo      Produced real insight
--     oo      identified real problems in the market
--  oo   Fast prototyping
--     oo      We could build quickly when direction was clear
--
--2.3 Which processes failed or slowed execution?
--  oo   No compliance-first thinking
--     oo      Led to rework and stalled progress
--  oo   No scope lock
--     oo      Everything important was treated as urgent
--  oo   No change control
--     oo      New ideas were added without removing old ones
--  oo   No delivery definition
--     oo      We didn't ship anything, the definition of delivery kept shifting.
--  oo   No stable execution cadence
--     oo      Progress was episodic, not compounding
--
--2.4 What workarounds emerged and why?
--Workaround 1: Big picture was mostly with Ceo
--  oo   Why: no structured synthesis or product system
--Workaround 2: Parallel product building
--  oo   Why: we had new insights on what the clients would need.
--Workaround 3: Manual reasoning instead of tooling
--  oo   Why: analyzer accuracy was not trustworthy
--Workaround 4: Over-expansion into l.m.s and notetaker
--  oo   Why: pressure to show progress despite lack of core readiness
--
--3) Measurement and Tracking
--3.1 How are we measuring success today?
--Reality:
--  oo   Informally
--  oo   Based on:
--     oo      what feels closer
--     oo      what demos better
--     oo      what unblocks conversation
--  oo   No single scorecard
--  oo   No weekly outcome tracking
--
--3.2 Which metrics actually influenced decisions?
--None consistently.
--Decisions were driven by:
--  oo   urgency
--  oo   intuition
--  oo   perceived opportunity
--  oo   external pressure
--
--3.3 Which metrics were tracked but not useful?
--  oo   We monitored development.
--These did not correlate with customer value or revenue.
--
--3.4 How do we track progress week to week?
--  oo   No consistent system
--  oo   No locked weekly outcome
--  oo   No falsifiable milestones
--
--Proposed Changes for 2026 (Product-Led)
--Goal changes
--  oo   One company-level outcome per quarter
--  oo   Outcome must include:
--     oo      delivery
--     oo      payment
--     oo      usage
--Process changes
--  oo   Consultancy-first delivery until product earns trust
--  oo   Hybrid delivery allowed but standardized
--  oo   Hard milestone ladder (binary, dated)
--  oo   Scope creep kill-switch
--Metric changes
--  oo   Weekly scorecard:
--     oo      facilities in motion
--     oo      delivered + paid
--     oo      cycle time
--     oo      analyzer error rate
--  oo   No metrics without decision consequence
--
--Final Truth (for the retreat)
--This is not a failure of intelligence or effort.
--It is a discipline gap:
--  oo   unclear goals
--  oo   undefined delivery
--  oo   unprotected scope
--  oo   late compliance awareness
--The Q1 2026 plan you now have directly corrects these failures.
--
--

--2026 Plans
--Theraptly  --  2026 Product Plan + okrs
--1) 2026 Product Thesis
--Theraptly earns trust by becoming legally defensible first, operationally repeatable second, and scalable only after controls and evidence are proven.
--Sequence: Hipaa readiness (defined) --> consultancy-led delivery + consultant self-serve --> ai hardening --> facility self-serve --> Notetaker scale --> soc 2 audit --> Billing
--
--2) Product Portfolio (Roles Locked)
--  1.   Policy Writer / Analyzer
--  oo   Q1: consultancy-led delivery + consultant self-serve
--  oo   Q2: facility self-serve begins
--  oo   Trust + revenue anchor
--  2.   lms
--  oo   Q1: finish + deploy + upsell
--  oo   Training evidence + expansion revenue
--  3.   Notetaker
--  oo   Q1: build + controlled pilots
--  oo   Q2: massive deployment
--  oo   Daily-use growth engine
--  4.   Billing Assistant
--  oo   Q3: build (pilot-only, high risk)
--  oo   Q4: validate (reduce rejected claims)
--  oo   Weekly-use retention driver
--
--3) Definitions (Non-Negotiable)
--3.1 "Hipaa compliant" in Q1 means this (and only this)
--Hipaa Q1 Readiness Definition (Hq1-r): Theraptly may claim "Hipaa-ready / Hipaa-aligned operations" only when all are true:
--  1.   Risk analysis completed and signed (v1, not draft)
--  2.   Required Hipaa policies live (privacy/security/breach + workforce training + sanctions)
--  3.   Access controls enforced (role-based access, least privilege, MFA where applicable)
--  4.   Audit logging live for Phi-touch events (access/upload/analysis/export/admin changes)
--  5.   Incident response + breach procedure tested once (tabletop exercise)
--  6.   Baa posture defined for all vendors that touch ePhi (or vendor removed)
--Explicit boundary: This is not a certification or government stamp. Sales-safe wording: "We operate with Hipaa-aligned controls and a completed risk analysis."
--3.2 42 Cfr Part 2 (Q1)  --  what it means operationally
--Part 2 readiness in Q1 means:
--  oo   Data classification exists for Part 2 programs
--  oo   Access restrictions and disclosure rules are enforced
--  oo   Staff trained on "extra-sensitive sud data"
--  oo   Outputs and notifications avoid sensitive metadata leakage
--3.3 soc 2 in 2026  --  timing locked
--  oo   Q2: audit readiness (controls operating + evidence)
--  oo   Q3: soc 2 Type I audit completed (mock type ii audit).
--
--4) A.i Engine Accuracy (Defined Properly)
--4.1 What is a "gap assertion" (unit of accuracy)
--A gap assertion is one claim of the form:
--"Policy set lacks requirement X" or "Requirement X is present but inadequate" or "Requirement X is present."
--4.2 What counts as accurate
--A gap assertion is accurate only if it is:
--  oo   Quote-anchored to exact text, or
--  oo   Correctly marked "Not found in uploaded documents", and
--  oo   Classified correctly (present / inadequate / not found)
--4.3 The Q1 accuracy target (99.9% defensible accuracy)
--99.9% defensible accuracy = >=99.9% of gap assertions in the test set are accurate by the definition above.
--4.4 Coverage floor (prevents "refuse to answer" gaming)
--The engine must produce a usable analysis for:
--  oo   >=95% of uploaded documents (coverage floor)
--4.5 Safety label requirement (until facility self-serve is mature)
--All exported reports in Q1 must include:
--  oo   "Tool-assisted analysis; final responsibility rests with reviewer"
--  oo   "No claim is made without quote anchoring or `not found'"
--
--5) Q1 Operating Model 
--Q1 Access Model (crucial)
--  oo   Facilities: receive deliverables; do not self-run analysis yet
--  oo   Consultants: get full self-serve access in Q1 (gated by onboarding + terms)
--Why this is the right ladder
--Consultants are controlled professional users; facilities are broader and higher-risk. Q1 focuses on trust, accuracy, and compliance evidence.
--
--
--6) 2026 Outcomes (One per Quarter)
--Q1 Outcome  --  Hipaa readiness + consultant self-serve + paid delivery
--By Mar 31, 2026, we operate at Hq1-r (Hipaa readiness defined above), deliver and collect payment for 10 facilities using Theraptly-run analysis + consultant self-serve, ship lms for upsell, and harden the ai engine to 99.9% defensible accuracy.
--Q2 Outcome  --  facility self-serve begins + Notetaker massive push
--By end of Q2, facility self-serve policy analysis is live safely, and Notetaker is deployed at scale with daily active usage.
--Q3 Outcome  --  soc 2 audit + Billing build
--By end of Q3, soc 2 Type I audit is completed successfully, and Billing Assistant v1 is piloted.
--Q4 Outcome  --  Billing validation + ecosystem expansion
--By end of Q4, Billing reduces rejected claims in pilots and multi-product adoption increases revenue per facility.
--
--7) Guardrails (Prevents 2025 repeat)
--7.1 Consultant self-serve gating (controls added)
--Consultants get access only after:
--  1.   30-minute onboarding (recorded)
--  2.   Terms accepted (use + disclaimers)
--  3.   First analysis reviewed once with pm/qa checklist (lightweight)
--  4.   Output labeling is non-removable in exports
--
--7.2 Facility self-serve (Q2) rollout kill-switch
--Facility self-serve cannot roll out if any of these occur in pilot:
--  oo   fabricated claim rate > 0%
--  oo   accuracy < 98.5%
--  oo   support burden > 10 tickets per 100 analyses
--  oo   incident involving Phi mishandling
--If triggered: rollback to consultant-only self-serve, facilities return to "deliverable-only" temporarily.
--7.3 pm load protection (hard rules)
--During Q1, pm cannot own everything. So:
--  oo   Design becomes Product Execution Arm
--     oo      templates, flows, onboarding screens, checklists, export formatting
--  oo   ai engineer owns accuracy work (pm defines specs + verifies)
--  oo   pm focuses on:
--     oo      definitions, gating, quality acceptance, delivery factory, weekly scorecard
--Kill-switch for overload: If ai accuracy is behind plan by >2 weeks, pm pauses new product asks (non-core) and reduces lms expansion tasks to "minimum sellable."
--7.4 Sales-safe compliance language (prevents overclaiming)
--Ceo and Growth must use approved phrases:
--  oo   ✅ "Hipaa-aligned controls and completed risk analysis"
--  oo   ✅ "soc 2 audit planned for Q3"
--  oo   ❌ "We are Hipaa certified"
--  oo   ❌ "We are fully compliant in every way"
--
--
--
--8) 2026 okrs 
--
--Q1 2026 okrs  --  Hipaa readiness + ai accuracy + consultant self-serve + lms
--Objective 1: Reach Hipaa Q1 readiness (Hq1-R) and Part 2 readiness
--Key Results
--  1.   Risk analysis v1 completed and signed.
--  2.   Hipaa + Part 2 workforce training completed by 100% of team.
--  3.   Audit logs live for access/upload/analysis/export/admin changes.
--  4.   Incident response + breach tabletop executed once with action items closed.
--
--Objective 2: Harden ai engine to 99.9% defensible accuracy with coverage
--Key Results
--  1.   >=99.9% accurate gap assertions on a test set of >=100 real policy documents.
--  2.   Coverage floor met: analysis succeeds on >=95% of uploaded documents.
--  3.   Fabricated claim rate = 0%.
--  4.   Manual correction rate <=10% of outputs by end of Q1.
--
--Objective 3: Deliver paid facility outcomes via Theraptly consultancy-led workflow
--Key Results
--  1.   10 facilities delivered + fully paid (contracts + evidence packs).
--  2.   Median delivery cycle time <=10 business days (upload complete --> delivered).
--  3.   100% of facilities receive corrected pack + change log + evidence pack.
--  4.   Ceo completes 10 sales cycles without compliance retraction.
--
--
--
--Objective 4: Make Consultant self-serve real, and lms sellable
--Key Results
--  1.   >=10 consultants onboarded (placeholders allowed) with gated access completed.
--  2.   80% MoM repeat usage among active consultants (activity definition locked).
--  3.   lms shipped + used in >=70% of delivered facilities within 30 days.
--  4.   lms training evidence included in >=80% of facility evidence packs.
--
--Q2 2026 okrs  --  Facility self-serve + Notetaker massive push
--Objective 1: Launch facility self-serve safely
--Key Results
--  1.   Facility self-serve enabled for policy analysis.
--  2.   >=50% of new analyses run directly by facilities.
--  3.   No degradation: accuracy stays >=98.5%, fabricated claims stay 0%.
--Objective 2: Deploy Notetaker at scale
--Key Results
--  1.   >=1,000 therapists onboarded.
--  2.   >=70% DAU/WAU.
--  3.   >=90% of recordings generate SOAP notes.
--  4.   NPS >=6.
--
--Q3 2026 okrs  --  soc 2 audit + Billing build
--Objective 1: Complete soc 2 Type I audit
--Key Results
--  1.   soc 2 Type I audit completed successfully.
--  2.   0 critical findings; <=3 minor findings.
--  3.   Remediation plan approved within 30 days.
--
--
--Objective 2: Build Billing Assistant v1 (pilot-only)
--Key Results
--  1.   Screen/context capture works reliably in target workflows.
--  2.   ai suggestions usable in >=2 billing workflows.
--  3.   Pilot launched in >=2 facilities.
--
--Q4 2026 okrs  --  Billing validation + ecosystem expansion
--Objective 1: Prove Billing impact
--Key Results
--  1.   Rejected claims reduced by >=20% in pilot facilities.
--  2.   Weekly usage by >=70% of pilot users.
--Objective 2: Increase revenue per facility
--Key Results
--  1.   >=60% of facilities use 2+ products.
--  2.   >=30% use 3+ products.
--  3.   ARPF up >=40% vs Q1. (Average Revenue per Facility)
