--Protected Sprint + Parallel Work + Compliance Gates + Hard Milestones + Timeline +
--RACI
--
--Quarter window: Jan 4 to Mar 31, 2026​
--Protected sprint window: Jan 12 to Feb 15, 2026​
--Sprint focus: Backend Policy Analyzer defensibility + h.i.p.a.a.-ready gate +
--consultant-operated workspace (consultant self-serve)
--
--0) Current truth (constraints)
--
--    ●​ Policy Writer is not in production and not tested end-to-end
--    ●​ Automated gap analysis is not production-grade
--    ●​ Infra is in-flight: a.w.s. to g.c.p. migration
--    ●​ Not yet h.i.p.a.a.-ready; formal risk analysis not complete
--    ●​ h.i.p.a.a. training starts early Q1; p.m records training video this week
--    ●​ New engineer joins for ai plus policy analyzer work during sprint start
--    ●​ No consultant workspace yet
--    ●​ l.m.s is about 70% done; notetaker about 40% done
--    ●​ Sales model:
--
--              ○​ Chad runs most facility calls and pipeline execution
--              ○​ Mr. Walker (Ceo) takes some facility calls plus partnerships, facility
--
--                   associations, executives, ex-execs, ex carf auditors
--    ●​ Operating posture for Q1: hybrid delivery is expected (system drafts + human
--
--         verification + corrected docs)
--
--1) Company outcome for Q1 (non-negotiable)
--
--By Mar 31, 2026, we close, deliver, and collect payment for 10 facility consultancy
--engagements end-to-end, using the Policy Writer workflow as the delivery system,
--with full evidence packs and no compliance retractions.
--2) Protected sprint objective (Jan 12 to Feb 15)
--
--By Feb 15, 2026, we have a backend-only Policy Analyzer that is 99.9% defensively
--accurate, reference-anchored, and trusted enough for Chad and Ceo led consultancy
--delivery and consultant-operated self-serve use.
--This sprint objective gates scale for the rest of Q1.
--
--3) What counts (definitions locked)
--
--3.1 Facility Active Use for Policy Writer (f.a.u.-p.w)
--
--A facility counts only when all are true:
--    1.​ Contract signed
--    2.​ Deposit collected (mandatory)
--    3.​ Policy inventory checklist agreed
--    4.​ At least 90% uploaded into the document hub
--    5.​ Gap report delivered (hybrid allowed)
--    6.​ Corrected policy pack delivered
--    7.​ Final payment collected
--    8.​ Evidence pack complete
--
--No evidence equals it didn’t happen.
--
--3.2 Deposit rule (mandatory)
--
--No deposit, no delivery slot. Work does not start until deposit is received.
--
--3.3 Upload deadline rule (mandatory)
--
--Upload due within 5 business days after deposit.​
--If not complete by Day 5:
--
--    ●​ delivery clock pauses
--    ●​ facility moves to “stalled upload”
--    ●​ delivery is rescheduled
--
--4) Q1 access model (controlled, non-negotiable)
--
--4.1 Consultants: consultant-operated workspace (Q1 enabled)
--
--Consultants can self-serve in Q1, but inside a constrained workspace.
--Consultants may:
--
--    ●​ upload policies
--    ●​ run analysis
--    ●​ export draft gap reports
--    ●​ generate correction drafts
--    ●​ manage multiple facilities they are assigned
--
--Consultants may not:
--
--    ●​ remove system labels or disclaimers
--    ●​ represent outputs as certification
--    ●​ override “not found” without human review trail
--    ●​ export without m.c.c plus change log attachment
--
--4.2 Facilities: no self-serve in Q1
--
--Facilities receive deliverables in Q1. Facility self-serve begins in Q2.
--
--5) What “99.9% accuracy” means (Policy Analyzer)
--
--5.1 Defensive accuracy rules (truthfulness guarantees)
--
--    ●​ The analyzer never invents policy content
--    ●​ The analyzer never asserts “your policy says” unless it can quote it
--    ●​ Every output item is either:
--
--              ○​ anchored to exact policy text (quote + location), or
--              ○​ explicitly “not found in uploaded documents”
--
--5.2 Measurement unit
--
--Accuracy is measured per gap assertion (not per document).
--
--5.3 Coverage floor (prevents refusal-gaming)
--
--Analyzer must produce usable output for at least 95% of uploaded documents.
--
--5.4 Mandatory output labeling in Q1
--
--Every export includes:
--
--    ●​ “tool-assisted draft, requires reviewer verification”
--    ●​ “no invented claims, quote anchored or not found”
--6) Deliverable quality gates (shipping rules)
--
--No deliverable ships unless all three pass:
--
--Gate A: truthfulness gate
--
--    ●​ fabricated claims rate = 0
--    ●​ quote-anchored or not found only
--
--Gate B: coverage gate (m.c.c)
--
--Each facility deliverable includes a Minimum Coverage Checklist with:
--
--    ●​ present and adequate
--    ●​ present but inadequate
--    ●​ not found
--
--Gate C: change-log gate
--
--Corrected policy pack includes a Correction Change Log:
--
--    ●​ what changed
--    ●​ where (policy and section)
--    ●​ why (gap addressed)
--    ●​ date and owner
--
--7) H.i.p.a.a.-ready in Q1 (definition + evidence gate)
--
--H.i.p.a.a.-ready by Q1 means all are true:
--
--    1.​ Risk analysis v1 completed and signed
--    2.​ Required h.i.p.a.a. policies live
--    3.​ workforce training completed by 100% of team
--    4.​ role-based access enforced
--    5.​ audit logs live for upload, access, analysis generation, export, and admin changes
--    6.​ incident response tabletop completed once with action items closed
--
--Sales-safe wording only:
--
--    ●​ “h.i.p.a.a.-aligned operations with completed risk analysis” Not allowed:
--    ●​ “h.i.p.a.a certified”
--    ●​ “fully compliant in every way”
--8) New guardrails added
--
--8.1 Accuracy grace band rule (keeps 99.9% without freezing progress)
--
--    ●​ Target remains 99.9%.
--    ●​ If accuracy is 99.7% to 99.89%, we do not pretend it is 99.9%.
--
--              ○​ We continue delivery only with mandatory secondary review on every report.
--              ○​ We do not expand consultant self-serve volume until 99.9% is achieved.
--    ●​ If accuracy is below 99.7%, protected sprint scope narrows further:
--              ○​ stop parallel distractions
--              ○​ fix top error patterns only until we return to grace band
--
--8.2 Consultant export traceability (liability hygiene)
--
--Every consultant export must contain:
--
--    ●​ consultant id
--    ●​ facility id
--    ●​ timestamp
--    ●​ analyzer version id
--    ●​ report version id
--
--Audit logs must record:
--
--    ●​ who exported
--    ●​ what was exported
--    ●​ when it was exported
--
--No export is considered valid without traceability.
--
--8.3 Sales ramp checkpoint (Jan 31)
--
--By Jan 31, we must have at least:
--
--    ●​ 4 proposals sent
--    ●​ 2 deposits collected
--
--If missed:
--
--    ●​ increase top-of-funnel immediately (more outbound, associations, partnerships)
--    ●​ Ceo temporarily increases deal involvement
--    ●​ reduce non-core internal work to protect sales throughput
--
--8.4 l.m.s stop condition (prevents silent failure)
--
--If l.m.s evidence is attached to fewer than 50% of delivered facilities by Facility 04, then:
--
--    ●​ l.m.s upsell pauses for 2 weeks
--    ●​ fix onboarding, reporting, and evidence packaging
--    ●​ resume upsell only after attachment rate recovers
--
--9) Strategy (how we win given constraints)
--
--    ●​ Consultancy sells now (Chad volume, Ceo strategic trust and partnerships)
--    ●​ Delivery is hybrid in Q1
--    ●​ Product stabilizes while delivering (stability beats features)
--    ●​ Compliance posture advances weekly, not later
--    ●​ Migration cannot pause sales or delivery (fallback lane exists)
--    ●​ l.m.s is completed and upsold in Q1 using minimum sellable definition
--    ●​ Notetaker continues build in Q1, massive push in Q2
--    ●​ s.o.c 2 audit is Q3, prep begins after Q1 stabilization
--
--10) What we will not do in Q1 (scope lock)
--
--    ●​ No facility self-serve policy analysis
--    ●​ No UI polish beyond functional basics
--    ●​ No “certification” claims (h.i.p.a.a or s.o.c 2)
--    ●​ No s.o.c 2 audit in Q1 (prep only)
--    ●​ No billing assistant work
--    ●​ No new major product expansions that do not unblock the 10-facility outcome
--
--11) Workstreams and owners (Q1)
--
--A) Consultancy delivery engine (Owner: p.m)
--    ●​ delivery factory
--    ●​ m.c.c, change log, evidence pack discipline
--    ●​ quality gates enforcement
--
--B) Policy Analyzer backend (Owner: ai engineer, accountable: eng lead)
--    ●​ parse, anchor, export, versioning
--    ●​ no guessing, no “helpful” speculation
--
--C) h.i.p.a.a readiness (Owner: compliance owner, supported by eng lead and p.m)
--    ●​ training, policies, risk analysis, logs, tabletop
--
--D) Sales and partnerships (Owner: Chad for pipeline, Ceo for strategic)
--    ●​ calls, proposals, deposits
--    ●​ association partnerships, auditors, exec networks
--
--E) l.m.s completion and upsell (Owner: l.m.s owner, support: design and p.m for evidence
--packaging)
--
--    ●​ minimum sellable definition
--    ●​ attach evidence to facility packs
--
--F) Notetaker (Owner: notetaker owner)
--
--    ●​ build progress in Q1
--    ●​ no massive deployment until Q2

--13) Fallback lane (migration cannot pause delivery)
--
--If g.c.p staging blocks delivery, delivery continues via one approved secure interim lane.
--
--Rules:
--
--    ●​ only one approved channel, no ad-hoc sharing
--    ●​ access limited to named roles
--    ●​ every file named with facility id, doc type, version
--    ●​ logged in evidence pack
--    ●​ imported into document hub once restored
--    ●​ removed from interim channel after import
--14) Weekly cadence
--
--Monday: targets, facility stage assignments, promise review (Ceo, Chad, p.m)​
--Tuesday: closing day (walkthroughs, deposits)​
--Wednesday: delivery day (p.m production, a.i fixes top error pattern)​
--Thursday: trust and payment day (delivery walkthroughs, final payment, referrals)​
--Friday: ship and retro (stability fixes, post-mortems)

--15) Timeline (Jan 5 to Mar 31)
--
--Jan 5 to Jan 12 (prep week)
--
--    ●​ corporate retreat
--    ●​ record l.m.s training video
--    ●​ lock analyzer “never do” rules
--    ●​ assemble policy corpus for testing
--    ●​ lock m.c.c categories and change-log template
--    ●​ publish sprint calendar
--
--Jan 12 to Feb 15 (protected sprint)

--THERAPTLY HIPAA COMPLIANCE PLAYBOOK
--
--1. Introduction
--
--Purpose: To ensure Theraptly complies with HIPAA while building and scaling its AI-driven compliance
--automation platform for behavioral health facilities.
--
--Scope: All systems, employees, contractors, and processes handling or potentially exposed to Protected
--Health Information (PHI).
--
--Status: Applies to MVP and beyond (dashboard, workspace, LMS, help center, admin).
--
--2. Key Definitions
--
--        • PHI: Any data that can identify a patient (names, IDs, health info).
--        • ePHI: PHI stored or transmitted electronically.
--        • Business Associate: A company (Theraptly) that creates, receives, maintains, or transmits PHI for
--
--          covered healthcare entities.
--        • BAA (Business Associate Agreement): Legal contract ensuring HIPAA compliance between
--
--          Theraptly and any party handling PHI.
--
--3. Core HIPAA Principles
--
--Rule                      Meaning for Theraptly
--
--Privacy Rule              Protect the confidentiality of all PHI handled by the system.
--
--Security Rule             Implement administrative, technical, and physical safeguards.
--
--Breach Notification Rule Report any unauthorized access or data breach within 60 days.
--
--Enforcement Rule          Maintain documentation for audits and investigations.
--
--4. Administrative Safeguards
--
--Goal: Ensure people, processes, and policies uphold privacy and security.
--
--        • HIPAA Compliance Officer: CEO (interim)
--        • Oversees policy implementation and staff compliance.
--        • Security Officer: Backend Tech Lead
--        • Maintains encryption, access control, and audit systems.
--        • Privacy Officer: Product Manager
--
--                                     1
--        • Ensures “minimum necessary” data handling in new features.
--
--Policies Required:
--
--        • Access Control Policy
--        • Incident Response & Breach Policy
--        • Employee Confidentiality Policy
--        • Vendor & BAA Policy
--        • Training & Awareness Policy
--        • Risk Assessment Policy
--
--Training:
--
--        • All team members complete HIPAA training annually.
--        • Confidentiality agreements signed by every employee and contractor.
--
--5. Technical Safeguards
--Goal: Protect data electronically at every layer.
--
--Area                Standard                          Theraptly Implementation
--                                                      All PHI in databases and storage encrypted
--Encryption (At Rest) AES-256                          (AWS S3, RDS).
--
--Encryption (In      TLS 1.2+                          All APIs and user traffic via HTTPS.
--Transit)
--Access Control      Unique IDs + RBAC                 Role-based permissions for users and staff.
--Authentication      MFA + Secure Tokens               Multi-factor for admin; session expiry.
--Audit Logs          Full Traceability                 Every upload, edit, download logged.
--Integrity Controls  Hashing & Versioning              Detect unauthorized changes to files.
--Transmission
--Security            Secure APIs                       Enforce signed requests, limited scopes.
--
--Data Backups        Daily Encrypted                   Stored in HIPAA-eligible region, 30-day
--                                                      retention.
--De-Identification   Remove PHI before AI
--                    processing                        ML pipeline sanitizes all text.
--
--6. Physical Safeguards
--        • Hosted on HIPAA-eligible cloud (AWS or Google Cloud) with signed BAA.
--
--                                                   2
--• Developer laptops encrypted (BitLocker / FileVault).
--• No local storage of PHI during development or testing.
--• Offboarding policy: revoke credentials immediately when someone leaves.
--
--7. Legal & Contractual Controls
--
--        • BAAs: Signed with all healthcare clients and any vendor touching PHI (AWS, chat provider, AI model
--          host).
--
--        • Privacy Policy: Publicly available, describing data use, storage, and deletion.
--        • Terms of Service: Defines user responsibilities for PHI protection.
--        • Incident Reporting: Template for notifying affected users and HHS within 60 days.
--
--8. Role-Based Responsibilities
--
--Role                      HIPAA Responsibilities
--
--CEO / Team Lead           Compliance Officer; sign BAAs; conduct annual risk analysis.
--
--Backend Tech Lead Security Officer; encryption, audit logs, backups.
--
--Frontend Engineer Prevent PHI caching; enforce secure sessions.
--
--UX Designer               Design privacy cues, consent modals, and least-exposure flows.
--
--Product Manager           Maintain data-flow map; approve features after HIPAA impact check.
--
--Growth / CX Officer Ensure HIPAA-compliant chat & marketing; redact PHI from feedback.
--
--ML Engineer               De-identify text; isolate embeddings; document data handling.
--
--9. Page-Level Safeguards
--
--Module                    HIPAA Focus   Control
--
--Login                     Authentication HTTPS only, MFA for staff.
--
--Dashboard / Doc Hub Secure storage AES-256 encryption; RBAC; audit logs.
--
--Upload Modal              Data ingress  TLS; validate file type; strip metadata.
--
--Workspace / Analyzer AI use of PHI      De-identify text; encrypt results; log views.
--
--Help / Chat               Support privacy HIPAA chat vendor; data retention limit.
--
--LMS (future)              Training content Generate from sanitized policies; encrypt learner data.
--
--Admin Panel               Internal control MFA; least privilege; log every change.
--
--                                        3
--10. Monitoring & Continuous Improvement
--        • Weekly: Log review by Security Officer.
--        • Monthly: Access audit and credential rotation.
--        • Quarterly: Backup restore test + internal mini-audit.
--        • Annually: Full HIPAA risk assessment + penetration test.
--
--11. Breach Response Plan
--      1. Detect → Contain → Document → Notify (within 60 days).
--      2. Notify affected customers and the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS).
--      3. Keep incident logs for 6 years.
--
--12. Future Preparations
--        • SOC 2 Type II or HITRUST certification once revenue supports it.
--        • Vendor review every 12 months.
--        • Publish a “Trust & Security” page summarizing controls for clients.
--        • Implement automated compliance scanning (CloudTrail, GuardDuty).
--        • Build de-identification API for future AI integrations.
--
--13. Appendices
--        • Risk-Assessment Template
--        • BAA Checklist
--        • Employee Training Record Sheet
--        • Incident Report Form
--        • Encryption Key Rotation Log
--
--                                                                           4
--

+++ /dev/null
@@@ -1,38 -1,38 +1,0 @@@
--QA Testing of Theraply LMS (Admin Interface)
--Summary
--I logged in as admin to the Theraply LMS site and explored the Documents, Training Center, Courses, and Staff Details sections to find any issues or broken functionality. The test environment uses a Supabase free tier backend, which affected user creation.
--Functional Issues Discovered
--Feature
--Issue observed
--Evidence
--Adding a new worker
--When trying to create a new worker and assign a course, an error message is displayed after clicking "Create Worker & Assign Courses." The error reads "Failed to create worker profile: Could not find the `must_change_password' column of `users' in the schema cache," which suggests a Supabase schema problem. The worker is not created.
--The failure message appears at the top of the page after attempting to create a new worker【235379352180572†screenshot】.
--Document categories  -  Add New
--In the Organization & Governance Documents section, there's an "Add New" button intended for adding a new document category. Clicking it does nothing; no modal or form appears, so new categories cannot be added.
--In the categories list, the Add New button in the upper right does not respond to clicks【641527189987042†screenshot】.
--Course creation (step 2)
--During course creation, after choosing a category (step 1) the process moves to step 2 to upload training documents. The "Next" button remains disabled until a file is uploaded, but there is no guidance or error message explaining why Next is disabled; the user might think the button is broken.
--On step 2 of course creation, the upload area is displayed and Next is disabled unless a document is uploaded【852902694636591†screenshot】.
--Navigation to subcategories
--Opening document categories sometimes requires double‑clicking instead of a single click, which feels inconsistent.
--To open a category such as Quality Management and Performance Improvement, I had to double‑click the category name before the page loaded【253552126410089†screenshot】.
--Staff details actions
--On the Staff Details page, each worker row has a three‑dot menu with only one option: "Remove Worker." There is no view or edit option. Additionally, removing a worker would trigger a destructive action; there's no confirmation modal shown (I did not proceed due to potential side effects).
--The action menu for a worker shows only a "Remove Worker" option【925445149106826†screenshot】.
--Upload progress for documents
--Each document category in Organization & Governance Documents and Clinical & Working Documents shows a badge labelled "Pending Upload." There's no way to mark a category as completed after uploading a document, so the status always remains "Pending Upload."
--The categories list shows every item with a yellow Pending Upload badge【641527189987042†screenshot】 even after opening the category and seeing a drag‑and‑drop upload field.
--Additional Observations
--File upload interface  -  Document categories use a drag‑and‑drop box limited to PDF/DOCX (max 100 MB). The interface looked clean, but I didn't upload a file, so I couldn't verify upload success or failure.
--Course assignment limitations  -  When opening an existing course's details page and clicking Assign, a modal says "All users are already assigned to this course." There's no way to add additional staff if none exist.
--Supabase free-tier limitations  -  The error about must_change_password indicates that the Supabase free tier schema is missing a column expected by the application. This prevents adding new users.
--Profile dropdown  -  The admin can open a dropdown from the top‑right profile avatar. It contains Edit Profile and Logout options, both of which appear functional; I did not test editing the profile or logging out.
--Recommendations
--Fix Supabase schema  -  Add the missing must_change_password column to the users table or update the code not to rely on it. This will allow worker creation to succeed.
--Implement "Add New Category"  -  Ensure the Add New button in the document categories list opens a form or modal to add a new category.
--Provide better user feedback  -  When steps require uploading a file (e.g., course creation), disable buttons only after giving clear instructions or tooltips explaining why the button is disabled.
--Consistent navigation  -  Use single-click activation for all links and list items to provide a consistent experience.
--More actions for staff  -  Add edit/view options to the staff actions menu and include a confirmation modal before permanently removing a worker.
--Update status badges  -  Automatically update a document category's status from Pending Upload to Uploaded when at least one document is added.
--These changes should improve the usability of the LMS and address the major bugs found during this QA session.

--Theraptly Audit-Readiness Overview
--1. Why Audit-Readiness Matters for Theraptly
--Even though Theraptly is a tech startup, it builds tools that touch regulated healthcare data and C.a.r.f.-accredited workflows. Behavioral-health facilities must be able to trust that using Theraptly will not put their own C.a.r.f. or H.i.p.a.a. compliance at risk. If the company seeks partnerships, contracts, or funding, auditors (internal or external) will want to verify that systems, staff, and policies follow best-practice standards.
--2. Types of Audits Theraptly Should Prepare For
--Audit Type
--Who Conducts It
--Purpose / Focus
--Timing
--H.i.p.a.a. Compliance Audit
--Independent compliance consultant or certified H.i.p.a.a. auditor
--Confirms that the A.i. platform, storage (A.w.s., S3), and staff procedures meet H.i.p.a.a. security and privacy rules.
--Once infrastructure is live and you begin handling client P.h.i.
--C.a.r.f. Alignment Review
--Internal audit by Compliance Officer (Product) or external C.a.r.f. specialist
--Validates that software logic, policy templates, and training modules correctly interpret and map to C.a.r.f. standards.
--Pre-launch and at every major update to policy database or training engine.
--Security / Cloud Infrastructure Audit
--Third-party cybersecurity firm or cloud partner (A.w.s. Audit Partner, Soc-2 assessor)
--Reviews access controls, encryption, network security, and backup integrity.
--After backend architecture is stabilized (6 - 9 months post-M.v.p.).
--Internal Process Audit
--Conducted by leadership (C.e.o., Engineering, Product.)
--Reviews adherence to internal P&P (governance, document control, role permissions).
--Quarterly or bi-annually.
--Investor / Due-Diligence Audit (future)
--Venture or grant auditors
--Verifies compliance maturity, risk management, and readiness to handle institutional clients.
--When pursuing large contracts or funding rounds.
--3. The Correct Order of Priority
--For Theraptly's stage (small team, early traction):
--1. Internal Readiness Audit (Quarterly): Conduct mock audits of document control, data access, and security.
--2. External H.i.p.a.a. Security Audit: Hire an independent consultant to assess technical and administrative safeguards.
--3. C.a.r.f. Alignment Validation: Work with a C.a.r.f. specialist or retired surveyor to review your database mappings.
--4. Who Should Lead Each
--Lead
--Responsibility
--Product / Compliance Lead
--Prepare internal P&P, manage C.a.r.f. policy mappings, document evidence.
--Engineering Lead
--Implement security controls (encryption, access logs, backups).
--M.L. Engineer
--Document A.i. data sources and model-training procedures for transparency.
--C.e.o.
--Approve and sign off on audit readiness reports, represent company during audits.
--5. What "Audit-Ready" Means for Theraptly
--:: Updated P&P library (the ones previously defined).
--:: Version-controlled evidence  --  logs, screenshots, training records, and signed policy reviews.
--:: Access logs & encryption proofs for all sensitive data.
--:: Incident logbook  --  even if "no incidents."
--:: Quarterly internal audit reports signed by the C.e.o.
--

--Policy and Procedure (P&P) Master Template
--
--(Designed for CARF/HIPAA-compliant behavioral health operations and easily adaptable for startups like Theraptly)
--
--1. Policy Information
--
--Field                  Details
--Policy Title           ☐ Administrative ☐ Clinical ☐ HR ☐ Safety/Security ☐ Information
--                       Management ☐ Financial/Billing ☐ Compliance
--P&P Type
--                       v1.0 / v1.1 / v2.0
--Policy Number
--Effective Date
--Review Date
--Approved By
--Responsible
--Department
--Version Control
--
--2. Policy Statement
--
--          A concise statement explaining what this policy ensures and why it exists. Connect it to
--          regulatory, ethical, or operational rationale.
--
--Example:
--This policy ensures that all staff adhere to HIPAA standards when handling Protected Health Information
--(PHI) to safeguard client privacy and organizational integrity.
--
--3. Purpose and Scope
--
--Purpose: Describe the goal or principle behind the policy (e.g., ensuring consistency, compliance, or risk
--mitigation).
--Scope: Specify who or what the policy applies to (e.g., all staff, clinicians, interns, contractors).
--
--                       1
--4. Definitions
--
--List and define key terms that need clarification.
--Example:
--
--        • PHI: Protected Health Information
--        • Breach: Unauthorized access or disclosure of confidential data.
--
--5. Procedure
--
--Step-by-step actions detailing how to implement the policy. Include roles, timeframes, and escalation paths.
--
--Example:
--
--      1. Staff must store all client files in encrypted folders (digital) or locked cabinets (physical).
--      2. Supervisors review access logs weekly.
--      3. Any suspected breach must be reported within 24 hours to the Compliance Officer.
--
--6. Roles and Responsibilities
--
--Role                           Responsibilities
--
--Compliance Officer Monitors adherence, investigates violations, updates policy as needed.
--
--Department Head Trains staff, ensures implementation in team workflows.
--
--All Employees                  Follow procedures, report deviations or incidents.
--
--7. Related Documents and References
--
--        • CARF Manual Section: [Insert applicable section]
--        • HIPAA Security Rule §164.306–316
--        • Organizational Code of Ethics
--        • Other Related Policies (list titles)
--
--8. Policy Review and Revision History
--
--      Version Date Changes Made                                            Approved By
--
--      v1.0                     [Date] Initial release                      [Name/Title]
--
--      v1.1                     [Date] Updated procedures                   [Name/Title]
--
--      v2.0                     [Date] Added new compliance section [Name/Title]
--
--                                                 2
--9. Compliance Grading (Optional Add-On)
--
--Criteria                      Compliant  Partially Compliant                     Non-Compliant  Notes
--                              (✓)        (⚠️)                                    (✗)
--Staff trained on this policy
--Procedure followed during
--audits
--Records properly
--documented
--
--10. Sign-Off
--
--                              Name Title Signature Date
--
--Template Use Guidance:
--
--        • Keep the Policy Statement under 5 lines—make it clear and purposeful.
--        • Ensure Procedures are testable and auditable.
--        • Each P&P should fit on 2–3 pages maximum.
--        • Attach forms or workflow diagrams separately when applicable.
--
--                                         3

--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--                      INTRODUCTION
--
--MCH wants to develop and maintain fair and supportive relationships with its employees. MCH
--personnel policies are in place to ensure consistency when addressing personnel issues. MCH
--strives to be in compliance with all applicable federal, state, and local regulations. MCH policies,
--practices, and benefits are regularly reviewed and updated as needed. MCH benefit plans are
--defined in legal documents such as insurance contracts, official plan texts, and trust
--agreements. Plan documents are available for employee inspection in the administrative office,
--or the employee may be provided with personal copies of benefit plans by the sponsoring
--company.
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                        4
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 1: Corporate Management
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--        DELEGATION OF RESPONSIBILITIES
--
--MCH is a private, non-profit corporation [501(c) 3] with a board of directors. Each employee is
--employed by Macon Citizens Habilities, Inc. (MCH). The executive director is directly
--responsible to the MCH Board and is charged with the supervision or delegation of supervision
--of all other personnel. MCH strives to assure that its board members and employees are
--leaders in the community and are qualified to carry out their assigned duties responsibly. The
--MCH Board authorizes the executive director to modify or add to MCH policies. The executive
--director is assisted in carrying out the overall responsibilities of the agency by the MCH
--management team comprised of the business director, HR manager, registered nurse, office
--manager, facility managers and qualified professionals.
--See Attachment 1—Organizational Chart
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                        5
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 1: Corporate Management
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--                      OPEN DOOR POLICY
--
--Employees are encouraged to share their concerns, seek information, provide input and resolve
--problems/issues through their immediate supervisors and, as appropriate, any member of
--management. Managers and supervisors are expected to be available to employees, listen to
--ideas and/or concerns and assist employees in resolving any problems or passing along input.
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                        6
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 1: Corporate Management
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--                   AT-WILL EMPLOYMENT
--
--MCH is an AT-WILL employer. At-will employment is a term used in U.S. labor law for
--contractual relationships in which an employee can be dismissed by an employer for any
--reason (that is, without having to establish "just cause" for termination), and without warning.
--While MCH policy encourages the use of progressive discipline to help employees correct poor
--work behavior, it does reserve the right to terminate employment at any time. Employees who
--have questions about the nature of their employment or the meaning of at-will policy should
--contact the executive director, business director or human resource manager.
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                        7
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 1: Corporate Management
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--       EQUAL EMPLOYMENT OPPORTUNITY
--
--MCH provides equal employment opportunities. We hire, train, promote and compensate
--employees on the basis of personal competence without regard for race, color, religion, sex,
--national origin, age over 40, marital status, disability or citizenship, as well as other classifica-
--tions protected by applicable federal, state or local laws.
--Equal employment opportunity applies to all aspects of employment with MCH including
--recruiting, hiring, training, transfer, promotion, job benefits, pay, dismissal, educational
--assistance, and social and recreational activities.
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                        8
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 1: Corporate Management
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--             EMPLOYEE CLASSIFICATIONS
--
--MCH has several job classifications which are based on position and defined by the Fair Labor
--Standards Act and other applicable laws or regulations. Employment positions are either
--exempt or non-exempt. Jobs are then classified as either full-time, part-time, temporary or
--PRN (as needed). Job classifications are stated in each job description.
--
--Exempt employees hold executive, administrative, professional or management positions.
--Exempt employees are salaried and are not eligible for overtime pay. Non-exempt employees
--are eligible for overtime pay and are paid overtime for all hours in excess of 40 hours worked
--per week. Exempt or non-exempt status is specified in the employee's job description. All
--employees whose regular scheduled shift is 30 hours or more each week is considered to be a
--full-time employee and eligible for MCH benefits. Any position that is regularly scheduled to be
--less than 30 hours/week is considered to be part-time and not eligible for MCH benefits, other
--than those mandated by law.
--
--While not anticipated, it may be necessary for MCH to hire a temporary worker to meet staffing
--or production needs. In any event, a temporary worker will not be employed for more than 6
--months (180 calendar days). Temporary workers are not eligible for any benefits other than
--mandated by law.
--
--PRN employees serve to help fill in shifts when there is a need for staff coverage in a MCH
--facility. PRN employees are cross-trained among different facilities. These employees work as
--needed and may choose whether they want to fill in hours when offered. PRN employees do
--not earn paid time off or receive holiday pay and are not eligible for ancillary insurance. PRN
--employees are eligible for overtime under the same parameters as all MCH employees. If a PRN
--employee is contacted and does not work at least 8 hours within a 90-day period, he or she will
--be removed from the PRN list. PRN employees must complete required training prior to
--starting to work as well as annually. PRN employees should not be used on third shift unless
--another trained MCH employee is available.
--
--Revised: 8/17; 8/19
--Reviewed: 4/18; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                        9
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 1: Corporate Management
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--         JOB DESCRIPTION AND ESSENTIAL
--                             FUNCTIONS
--
--Every job within MCH has a detailed description which states the essential functions and
--primary responsibilities of the position. The essential functions are those functions which are
--the primary purpose of the position. A written job description is provided at the time of
--employment application and reviewed during the interview as well as when an employee is
--hired. Essential functions are often determined by service definitions, applicable state and
--federal laws and department of labor recommendations.
--Employees must meet the qualifications and minimum requirements for the position held or
--desired including education, skills, work experience, licenses, certifications, physical and mental
--abilities, health and safety, judgment, interpersonal skills or other job-related requirements.
--Employees must be able to perform all functions of the position, essential and non-essential.
--See Attachment—Job Description and Job Analysis Receipt
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                      10
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 1: Corporate Management
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--          REASONABLE ACCOMMODATIONS
--
--A reasonable accommodation is assistance with or changes to job duties or the workplace that
--will enable an employee with a disability to do his or her job. MCH will make every effort
--provide reasonable accommodations to a qualified employee who cannot perform non-
--essential job functions because of a disabling condition. The reasonable accommodation must
--not pose an undue hardship on MCH and the employee must not pose a direct threat to the
--health and safety to their self or others. Employees and applicants wanting to request a
--reasonable accommodation should do so in writing and present request to their supervisor or
--HR Manager. MCH may ask for additional documentation such as a note from a doctor or
--specialist to ensure the accommodation request is adequate.
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--

--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel

--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--                          JOB POSTINGS
--
--Available positions are posted on a bulletin board in the administrative office and on the MCH
--website at www.maconcitizens.org. A list of available positions is provided to each residential
--facility and the day program. If qualified for the position, current employees may apply for any
--job posted by completing page 1 of the MCH application.
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20

--                                CHANGE
--

--for the position, current employees interested in applying need to complete page 1 of the MCH
--application. Every effort will be made to promote or transfer current staff in good standing
--when a desired position opens. The HR Manager, facility manager(s), qualified professionals
--and/or executive director will make final decisions based the best interests of the persons
--served who may be impacted by the change.

--
--    INTRODUCTION to ETHICAL STANDARDS
--
--MCH takes every measure possible to conduct its business activities with integrity, fairness and
--in accordance with the highest ethical standards. Employees are expected to do their jobs with
--the same level of integrity. If ever in doubt whether an activity meets our ethical standards or
--compromises MCH's reputation, employees should discuss it with a supervisor or the executive
--director.
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20

--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 2: Ethical Standards
--
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--                        CODE OF ETHICS
--
--MCH employees have an obligation to do more than meet legal standards and regulations: all
--MCH staff are expected to exercise standards of conduct as well. Political parties and special
--interest groups are not endorsed by this organization. MCH actively encourages every
--employee to recognize and report any concern about possible illegal or unethical behavior.
--Such reports may be made in good faith and will be explored responsibly and without
--retaliation. MCH and its employees will adhere to the following practices:
--
--    • Represent the interests of all people served by this organization and not favor special
--         interests inside or outside of MCH
--
--    • Keep confidential information confidential including personal health information
--    • Maintain the trust of persons served and/or their families/guardians
--    • Maintain the trust of the community
--    • Focus efforts upholding MCH’s mission statement
--    • Refrain from entering into business transactions with persons served by MCH or using
--
--         their funds for personal gain
--    • Use billed or donated revenue for the purpose intended by the funding source
--    • Refrain from engaging in other employment, business relationships or services that pose
--
--         a conflict of interest with MCH or the services it offers
--    • Ensure that all marketing materials are accurate, correctly reflect the MCH’s mission
--    • objective and are not in conflict with any local, state or federal regulations
--    • Ensure donors are informed about the value and tax implications of any contributions
--
--         and that contributions are used in accordance with donors’ intentions
--    • Carry out work responsibilities and provide services in an honest and diligent manner
--
--         within the scope of all labor laws
--    • Ensure all activities related to the management of MCH adhere to all statutory acts,
--    • regulations or by-laws which relate to the field, as well as all civil and criminal laws,
--    • regulations and statutes that apply in their jurisdiction. Management will not knowingly
--
--         or otherwise engage in or condone any activity or attempt to circumvent the clear
--    • intention of the law
--    • Provide accurate information on job applications, resume or in the ordinary conduct of
--
--         affairs, claim and never imply a degree of competency he/she does not possess.
--    • Ensure all documentation and communication is accurate and timely
--    • Communicate ethical expectations to all employees, stakeholders and board members
--
--         as well as provide opportunity for feedback, questions and concerns to resolve ethical
--         issues
--    • Refrain from accepting individual gratuities
--
--                                                                                                                                      15
--    • Allow legal guardians to work for MCH but not in the same facility as the person for
--         whom they are responsible. Exceptions to this are only made for some recipients of NC
--         Innovations services
--
--    • Spouses, domestic partners, nor relatives may not work in the same facilities with the
--         exception of certain NC Innovations services.
--
--    • Treat all people with dignity and respect
--    • Ensure information posted on social networks is of a positive nature and does not
--
--         violate any ethical or confidential standards
--The Code of Ethics is reviewed by members of MCH management annually and approved by the
--board. Each MCH employee must acknowledge his/her commitment to this Code of Ethics at
--time of hire and at least annually thereafter. Documentation is kept in the employee record.
--Any employee who deliberately or knowingly violates the MCH Code of Ethics is subject to
--review by the executive director and/or the board of directors and may be subject to
--termination. Any allegation of ethical misconduct will be investigated immediately by members
--of management and/or the board of directors. Results of any investigation will be documented
--and maintained in the personnel file and be shared with the board of directors. Substantiations
--of ethical misconduct are treated according to the seriousness of the infraction and may result
--in reprimand, suspension, or termination.
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--

--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 2: Ethical Standards
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--                  CONFLICT OF INTEREST
--
--MCH is a non-profit, tax-exempt organization. In order to maintain this status, the IRS as well
--as state regulatory and tax officials review the operations of MCH as a public trust. Because of
--this, there is a fiduciary duty between MCH and its employees, the board of directors and the
--public that carries the responsibility of managing MCH and its practices in a fair and honest
--manner. No MCH employee, board member, person served or their immediate family shall
--benefit unfairly or unlawfully from any activity conducted by MCH or from any funds received
--or purchases made by MCH. Conflicts may occur with persons or businesses that contract with,
--donate to or have any operational association with MCH.
--Any real, apparent, or alleged conflict of interest shall be made known in writing to the
--executive director who will in turn make the MCH Board of Directors aware as soon as possible.
--Exceptions may be granted by the board of directors, particularly if a competitive bid or
--comparable valuation exists. Any actions or exceptions will be recorded in the board minutes
--and reported to appropriate parties or agencies as necessary. If the alleged conflict of interest
--involves the executive director, the person making the allegation should report directly to the
--president of the board of directors.
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                      17
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 2: Ethical Standards
--
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--             CORPORATE RESPONSIBILITY
--
--Corporate responsibility refers to the way we carry out the values and principles that govern
--how we operate as an organization and behave as individuals. Corporate responsibility is how
--we ensure safe work operations, create a positive impact on the people we serve, fellow
--coworkers and the communities we work in. MCH strives to conduct business and provide
--services that build trust and respect from the persons we serve and our stakeholders. MCH
--makes every effort to eliminate waste, provide liability protection and reduce civil and criminal
--exposure. MCH has a coordinated, organized approach to manage the complex laws and
--regulations which govern its services, as well as conditions of participation for state and
--Medicaid programs and independent accrediting bodies.
--
--All MCH employees share a responsibility for ensuring that any interaction with the people we
--serve, their families, colleagues, shareholders, contractors or associates are done with honesty,
--integrity, openness and professionalism. Since 1988, MCH has demonstrated responsible
--conduct and public service to the developmentally disabled which attributes to our success as a
--provider. Based on our history and commitment, MCH ensures the people we serve have the
--benefit of trained, nurturing staff who provide services that exceed standard expectations as
--well as offer facilities that are clean and well-maintained. Families and guardians entrust MCH
--staff with providing service to their family member. MCH expects its staff to reciprocate by
--being trustworthy and conducting themselves with utmost integrity at all times. The executive
--director is responsible for ensuring all MCH operations are conducted accordingly and reports
--to the board of directors at least quarterly.
--
--Corporate responsibility is enforced by the monitoring of the following:
--    • Policies and procedures
--    • Staff training
--    • Internal controls
--    • Communication and reporting
--    • Investigation
--    • Disciplinary actions
--    • Self-review for compliance with federal, state and local laws and regulations
--
--By offering education about compliance with laws and regulations that impact MCH in risk-
--sensitive areas, MCH demonstrates corporate responsibility, as well as appropriate due diligence
--and a good faith effort to comply with these laws and regulations. Contract affiliates are
--informed of requirements of corporate responsibility through the contracting process. Both
--annual reviews of corporate compliance and risks contribute to being corporately responsible.
--
--                                                                                                                                      18
--Current auditing policies, including an independent outside financial audit and quarterly audits
--by the compliance officer, provide a solid basis for assessment and monitoring. Review of
--changing laws and regulations are reflected in changes to organizational policies and
--procedures. The executive director and upper management team have the authority to develop
--and monitor policies to support any and all elements of compliance. Policy changes are noted
--by date in the Policies, Procedures, and Operations Manual. State-initiated, unannounced
--annual survey results provide feedback for corporate responsibility and provide measurement
--for compliance to regulations.
--The executive director is responsible to investigate any report of misconduct, non-compliance to
--standards, or illegal action. The executive director may delegate certain investigative tasks.
--Legal consultation may be obtained if necessary. It is the responsibility of the executive director
--to submit appropriate plans of correction or make other arrangements to bring programs into
--compliance. In the event that allegation is made about the executive director, the president of
--the board of directors will direct the investigation.
--Each investigation includes the gathering and preservation of relevant documents and
--identification and interviewing of employees, former employees, and others who may be able
--to provide pertinent information. Documentation should include a report describing the
--disclosure, the investigation process, the conclusions reached, and the recommended
--corrective action, where such action is necessary. When a potential violation of law is
--identified, legal counsel may be sought. Any necessary actions to correct the violation will be
--implemented immediately. MCH will report corrective actions to the government on a voluntary
--disclosure basis. If needed, MCH policies may be modified to prevent further violations.
--Disciplinary actions, if needed, will be implemented according to MCH policy.
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                      19
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 2: Ethical Standards
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--                         CONSERVATION
--
--As individuals and an agency, we have a responsibility to safeguard the environment for present
--and future generations. To practice this responsibility, MCH has conservation standards within
--the organization. Recycling and energy efficiency are 2 of the simplest ways to have a positive
--impact on the world in which we live. The following procedures outline practical ways to
--conserve within our facilities and while providing services:
--
--    • Do not overheat rooms in the winter. Set the thermostat to 70 degrees
--    • Do not overcool rooms in the summer. Set the thermostat to 72 degrees
--    • Pull shades or curtains at night to keep heat in and cold out on cold evenings
--    • Do not waste water. Turn off the tap while brushing teeth and shaving
--    • Separate recyclables and take to recycling centers
--    • Recycle all aluminum cans at MCE
--    • Use washable napkins rather than paper napkins when possible
--    • Use only the amount of paper supplies that is needed
--    • Clean or replace air filters as recommended
--    • Recycle printer cartridges
--    • Use compact fluorescent light bulbs when possible
--    • When possible and if approved, use green chemicals for maintenance and cleaning
--    • Use reusable bags when shopping
--    • Compost when possible
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                      20
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 3: Employment
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--          APPLICATION FOR EMPLOYMENT
--
--MCH intends to recruit, hire, and place applicants on the basis of the applicant’s relative
--knowledge, skills, and abilities. The decision to employ an applicant is based on the person’s
--qualifications for the particular position along with other requisite job skills. Minimum
--qualifications are specified in the job description. Applicants must meet the minimum
--qualifications for the job for which they are applying. Applicants should provide a high school
--diploma, equivalency, or college degree or license if required by the position. Professional
--positions require transcripts or licenses.
--
--Applicants should complete an MCH application and submit it to the HR manager. A resume
--may not substitute for an application. All employees of MCH are subject to a background and
--criminal record check if employed after July, 1992. Employees hired after March, 1998 are
--subject to a test for controlled substances. Any applicant who provides transportation is also
--subject to a MVR background check. If the position requires the employee to use their personal
--vehicle while working, the applicant must also provide proof of liability insurance
--(100,000/300,000 minimum coverage). All direct care staff will have Healthcare Personnel
--Registry checks. References are checked and documented. Applicants or new hired employees
--must present the following:
--
--    • completed application
--    • current references
--    • a copy of diploma or equivalency (as required for the position)
--    • certified transcripts (if applicable for the position)
--    • copies of any necessary certifications or licenses (as required by the position)
--    • current driver’s license (as required for the position) or proof of age 18
--    • car insurance with proof of minimum coverage requirement (if required for the position)
--
--If needed documentation cannot be provided, MCH will not offer the position.
--
--See Attachment—Application for Employment
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                      21
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 3: Employment
--
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--         INTERVIEWING FOR EMPLOYMENT
--
--MCH observes sound interviewing and hiring practices that conform to applicable federal, state,
--and regulatory requirements. MCH intends to recruit, hire, and place applicants on the basis or
--the applicant’s knowledge, skills, and abilities. The decision to employ an applicant is based
--solely on the individual’s qualification for the particular position along with other requisite job
--skills. Minimum qualifications are specified in the job description.
--
--Procedures:
--    1. Upon receipt in the administrative office, an application for employment is stamped
--         with the date received and submitted to the HR manager.
--    2. The HR manager reviews current applications with the respective QP to ensure the
--         applicant meets job requirements and is available for the identified shift.
--    3. The HR manager schedules an interview with the applicant.
--    4. The HR manager and QP or at least 1 other person conduct the interview with an
--         approved set of interview questions.
--    5. During the interview process, the following topics are to be reviewed with the applicant:
--         • benefits including leave and retirement and insurance
--         • salary
--         • pay period
--         • confidentiality
--         • mandatory staff meeting attendance
--         • criminal record checks
--         • drug screening
--         • requirement for a valid NC driver’s license and any violations
--         • documentation of high school graduation
--         • secondary employment
--         • training and orientation
--         • tobacco-free, scent and drug-free environment and dress code
--         • offensive behavior
--         • medical history if offered a job
--         • FCRA Rights Summary
--         • sleep time exclusionary agreement
--         • 2-step PPD
--    6. The consents and releases are obtained which include criminal background check, List of
--         Excluded Individuals/Entities (LEIE) check, consent to release information for criminal
--         and MVR records, consent to fingerprinting and national record check (if applicable***),
--
--                                                                                                                                      22
--         consent to release Information to MCH to check references, release to visit facility, sleep
--         time exclusionary agreement (if applicable), non-disclosure and confidentiality
--         agreement and HIPAA statement
--    7. Following the interview, a visit to the facility is scheduled with the applicant to meet the
--         person(s) served and manager for their approval. If the position is for a family in the
--         community, the applicant will meet with the family and person served for their
--         approval.
--    8. The applicant must inform the HR manager within 24 hours of visit to a facility if
--         interested in the position.
--    9. When the applicant confirms continued interest in the position, the HR manager may
--         offer the job contingent on results from observations and the previously mentioned
--         observations, checks and screenings. These results are maintained in the personnel
--         record if employment is offered.
--
--See Attachment—Confidentiality Agreement
--See Attachment—Interview Questionnaire
--See Attachment—Summary of Your Rights Under the Fair Credit Reporting Act
--See Attachment—Pre-Employment Disclosure for Consumer Reports
--See Attachment—Pre-Employment Authorization for Consumer Reports
--See Attachment—Personal Data for Criminal and MVR Background Check
--See Attachment 44—Authority for Release of Information National Record Check and Applicant
--Request for Criminal History Record Information***
--See Attachment—Consent to Release Information to MCH (Employee)
--See Attachment—Visiting MCH Facility Release
--See Attachment—Sleep Time Exclusion Agreement
--See Attachment—Medical History Inquiry and Release

--Employees/Applicants

--See Attachment—List of Excluded Individuals/Entities (LEIE) Check

--***Per G.S. 122C-80 (b), if the applicant has not lived in NC for 5 consecutive years, a federal criminal record check
--is done by the State Bureau of Investigation and includes fingerprinting

--                SECONDARY EMPLOYMENT
--
--MCH expects to be a full-time employee’s primary job. Secondary employment cannot conflict
--with job performance or create a conflict of interest. Other employment must have prior
--written approval of the HR manager, business director or executive director. Before accepting
--employment with MCH, a prospective employee must assure that a secondary job will not
--interfere with their MCH job duties, including attendance of mandatory staff meetings.
--See Attachment—Approval for Secondary Employment

--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--                TRAINING REQUIREMENTS
--
--MCH provides a comprehensive orientation and mandatory training during the first several days
--of employment. Training complies with or exceeds state requirements. Employees are
--monitored by supervisors, managers and professional staff for compliance. Some courses are
--offered online while others are directly with an MCH trainer. Depending on the course, staff
--have to demonstrate their understanding of the training material by passing a test and/or
--demonstrating proficiency in action. All MCH staff who serve as trainers meet requirements as
--outlined by the course, licensing, regulation and/or 10 NCAC 27G.0203, 0204.
--
--Annual refresher training is mandatory as required by state and federal regulations. Failure to
--complete annual training within the allotted time period is subject to suspension without pay
--on work days until the training is complete. Unless scheduled by management, training should
--not be completed while an employee is working their regularly scheduled shift.
--
--Employee training shall consist of the following:
--    • rights of the persons served and confidentiality as mandated in 10A NCAC 27C, 27D,
--         27E, 27F and 10A NCAC 26B
--    • individual needs/outcomes as outlined in the person’s service plan
--    • infectious diseases and blood borne pathogens
--    • OSHA training including orientation to postings
--    • First Aid
--    • CPR
--    • signs and symptoms of illness
--    • North Carolina Interventions (NCI)
--    • procedures for identifying, reporting, investigating and controlling infectious and
--         communicable diseases of personnel and clients
--    • hazard communication
--    • ergonomics
--    • emergency and fire safety including hands-on use of fire extinguishers
--    • defensive driving
--    • lift van, if applicable
--    • prevention of violence in the workplace
--    • safe practices including slips, trips, falls, back safety, hazards, evacuation and
--    • emergencies
--    • active treatment
--    • documentation
--
--                                                                                                                                      26
--    • reporting and communication
--    • medication administration
--    • unlawful harassment
--    • corporate responsibility
--    • service-specific competencies as applicable
--    • MCH Policies and Procedures
--New employees are provided a MCH Employee Handbook which is reviewed with the employee
--by the HR manager at the time of hire. This handbook supplements the policies located in this
--manual. A copy of the Employee Handbook is available online and in each licensed facility.
--Each direct care employee has a written supervision plan which is monitored by a qualified
--professional staff. MCH maintains documentation of initial and annual training according in
--personnel files. Documentation must include the date and brief description of the training,
--score (if applicable) and the instructor’s name. Most training occurs within the first 2 - 4 weeks
--of employment. Documentation of some training from another agency may be considered in
--lieu of MCH training on a case by case basis.
--Employees are encouraged to take advantage of any additional training available through
--College of Direct Supports (online training module) and Therap (electronic health record).
--Employees will be compensated for time spent taking non-required trainings, so long as the
--subject of the course relates to the employee’s job duties with MCH.
--See Attachment—New Employee Orientation and Training
--See Attachment—In-Service Training Sign-Up Sheet
--See Attachment—Employee Handbook
--
--Revised: 8/17; 7/20
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19

--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 3: Employment
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--                CONTRACTED PERSONNEL
--
--It is often fiscally responsible to enter into a contractual relationship with a professional than to
--employ a full-time person to fulfill certain obligations. MCH contracts with several
--professionals to provide consultation for the people we serve.
--MCH holds its consultants and contracted business associates to the same high standards of
--conduct as other staff. Consultants must provide the organization with proof of credentials and
--current licenses. Copies of licenses and certificates of insurance are maintained in a file in the
--administrative office with the signed contract. Contracted personnel are subject to
--performance reviews each year to determine if they have fulfilled the responsibilities of their
--contracts. These reviews are kept within the consultant’s file.
--Each contracted personnel enters into a legal agreement with MCH. Contracts include the
--specific responsibilities of the consultant or business associate and the specific responsibilities
--of MCH. Contracts include confidentiality, MCH Code of Ethics, Corporate Responsibility and a
--business associates agreement. Contracts are time-limited and contain a provision for ending
--the contract. Contracts become valid when signed in good faith by both parties.
--See Attachment—Business Associates Agreement
--See Attachment—Consultant Performance Review
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
--                                                                                                                                      28
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 3: Employment
--
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--      EMPLOYEE APPEALS AND GRIEVANCE
--
--MCH employees are able to file grievances at their discretion when they feel wrong-doing has
--occurred. The following is a step by step process an employee must follow to get his or her
--complaint addressed:
--
--    1. An employee should present their grievance to his/her immediate supervisor for
--         discussion. The supervisor has 5 regular working days to respond.
--
--    2. If the supervisor fails to respond within 5 working days or if the employee finds the
--         response unsatisfactory, the appeal may be put in writing, clearly specifying the policy
--         allegedly misapplied and the relief requested. The appeal should be submitted to the HR
--         manager within 5 regular working days from the time the first step answer was due or
--         was given.
--
--    3. The HR manager should respond in writing within 5 working days after receiving the
--         grievance. If the HR manager fails to respond within this time, if the employee finds the
--         response unsatisfactory, or in cases where the HR manager is the immediate supervisor,
--         the employee may submit an appeal to the executive director.
--
--    4. Upon receipt of a written appeal, the executive director will inform the board chair of
--         the appeal.
--
--    5. The employee shall receive an answer in writing from the executive director no later
--         than 15 working days from receipt of the written grievance or be advised as to the
--         conditions which prevent an answer within 15 working days. If it has to take longer than
--         15 days for an answer, the executive director must provide a reason as to why.
--
--    6. If the discussion between the executive director and the employee is not satisfactory to
--         the employee, the employee may request in writing that the MCH Board consider the
--         matter. The request to the Board must be within 2 working days of the answer from the
--         executive director.
--
--    7. The executive director shall provide the board with all pertinent information.
--    8. The MCH Board will review the facts with the executive director and seek any other
--
--         sources of information or consultations necessary. At this step, arrangements will be
--         made for the employee to review his/her case with the MCH Board if the employee so
--         desires.
--    9. The employee must present to the board the grievance personally and without
--         assistance of representation other than a currently employed co-worker. Grievances
--         shall be heard only in executive session at the next regularly scheduled meeting.
--    10. The board president will notify the employee of the decision and provide their decision
--         in writing. This decision will be kept within the personnel file.
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--

--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--                            PAY PERIOD
--
--MCH pays on a standard bi-weekly basis. A schedule for pay periods is provided annually to all
--MCH facilities and staff. When the scheduled payday falls on a holiday, payday is the first
--working day following the holiday.
--Only the employee or someone authorized by the employee in writing may pick up a paper
--paycheck/stub. Written authorization should be presented by the employee prior to the
--payday; authorization will be maintained in the personnel file. A check will not be released
--without written authorization.
--
--Revised: 8/17; 7/19
--Reviewed: 4/18; 7/20

--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--                           TIME SHEETS
--
--Hourly employees who work in a MCH facility are responsible for documenting their beginning
--and ending of their shift on time sheets. If an employee fails to document their time, they must
--contact their supervisor and either the business director or administrative office manager. Any
--late submissions of time worked will be reimbursed on the next time period. Time sheets must
--be completed on a daily basis as hours are worked.

--
--                        WORK SCHEDULE
--
--Generally, the standard work week is 40 hours for full-time hourly employees. The immediate
--supervisor will provide a schedule. Work weeks for salaried employees is as needed
--to complete the responsibilities of the job. The work week begins at 12:00 midnight on Sunday
--and ends at 11:59 PM on Saturday.

--                              OVERTIME
--
--All hourly employees are eligible for overtime pay. Overtime must be authorized by a su-
--pervisor. Overtime rate is paid according to federal law and offered at a 1 ½ times the
--employee’s regular hourly salary. All hours worked in excess of 40 per work week will be paid
--as overtime.

--                                                                                                                                      34
--                                                                                        MCH Policy & Procedures
--                                                                                        Section 2: Personnel
--                                                                                        Part 3: Employment
--
--__________________________________________________________________________________
--
--     IMPROPER OR UNLAWFUL DEDUCTIONS
--                              FROM PAY
--
--Every effort is made to ensure that compensation and pay checks are properly computed and
--calculated. Employees can report improper or unlawful deductions from their wages without
--fear of discrimination or reprisal. Upon receiving notification of an improper or unlawful
--deduction from pay, the business director will investigate the matter and issue a finding before
--the next pay period. If the investigation confirms the deduction was improper or unlawful, the
--employee shall be reimbursed the amount of the deduction on the next pay check. Incidents
--should be reported to the business director within 1 week of the pay date.
--
--The salaries of employees who meet exempt status under the Fair Labor Standards Act, 29 CFR
--Part 541 may be reduced or be subject to deduction for the following conditions only:
--
--    • one or more full days of absence for personal reasons other than sickness or disability
--         and the employee has no leave to cover the absence
--
--    • for sickness or disability (including work place injury) if the employee has not qualified
--         for PTO, has not earned sufficient PTO to cover the absence, or has exhausted all PTO
--         and has no earned leave remaining to cover the absence. If the employee has
--         exhausted all PTO to cover an FMLA absence, the employee’s salary may be reduced in
--         hourly increments while on FMLA leave
--
--    • deductions for penalties imposed for violations of safety rules of major significance,
--         including those relating to the prevention of serious danger in our workplace or to other
--         employees
--
--    • deductions resulting from suspensions without pay for serious violations of our
--         workplace misconduct rules
--
--    • in the initial or final work week of employment, deductions may be made for the days of
--         the workweek not worked. For example, in the first or last week of employment, if the
--         employee only works 2 of the 5 days, the employee will receive 2/5 (two fifths) of the
--         weekly salary. In the final workweek the employee may use applicable accrued leave to
--         cover the portion of the week not worked but only as provided elsewhere in these
--         policies
--
--Deductions from salaries of employees who meet exempt status under the Fair Labor Standards
--Act, 29 CFR Part 541, are NOT permitted by the regulation for the following conditions:
--
--    • on an hourly basis except for unpaid FMLA leave and as provided in the special rules
--         above.
--
--                                                                                                                                      35
--    • when the office or facility is officially closed for all employees by the executive director
--         due to inclement weather such as snow or ice
--
--    • for penalties or rules violations such as performance issues, attendance issues, minor
--         safety rules, cash shortages, losses, rules of evidence violations or damages to
--         equipment or property, including insurance deductibles when damage has occurred
--
--Revised: 8/17
--Reviewed: 4/18; 8/19; 7/20
--
